source.title: quickcheck: add lightweight oncall quick-check CLI and compact diagnostic packer
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description: Implements a small, dependency-light quick-check CLI and a compact diagnostic packer that incident responders can run from the incident channel or a runbook step. Motivation: many oncall incidents are prolonged by manual ad-hoc data collection and noisy attachments. This PR provides a safe, opinionated tool that captures a bounded set of artifacts (sanitized logs, pod status, recent traces, KV cache hints) and emits a single compressed artifact suitable for attaching to an incident thread or uploading to the internal diagnostics bucket. Key properties: fast (<= 15s typical), bounded output size (configurable default 8MB), privacy-first sanitization hooks, dry-run mode, and automatic S3-compatible upload with short-lived presigned URLs. Includes a new slash-command integration for incident-bot (/quickcheck) to trigger the CLI remotely via action runners and a small Webhook consumer used by private deployments. See linked Linear tickets ENG-3267 and ENG-3312 for rollout plan. Checklist: [x] Quick-check CLI implementation, [x] packer sanitizers and size enforcement, [x] slash-command wiring and permission checks, [x] small tutorial in docs/runbooks/quickcheck.md, [x] e2e smoke test harness, [ ] optional telemetry opt-in toggle (follow-up).
source.commits: 3 commits: 1) add quickcheck CLI and core packer, 2) add sanitizers, size-limiter and upload adapter, 3) wire slash-command and docs. Commit messages include minimal reproduction steps and test notes.
source.changed_files: incident-bot/cmd/quickcheck/main.go, incident-bot/pkg/packer/packer.go, incident-bot/pkg/packer/sanitizers.go, incident-bot/pkg/uploader/s3_adapter.go, incident-bot/api/slash_commands.go, docs/runbooks/quickcheck.md, test/e2e/quickcheck_smoke_test.sh, scripts/ci/quickcheck_integration.yml and small README/Makefile updates.
source.review_comments: Samir: please ensure the default sanitizer list covers PII keys we know about, noted a missing AWS creds fallback in private deploys. Elena: suggested tightening the RBAC check for slash-command to require oncall role and not channel-level perms. Matt: requested a size enforcement test to avoid oversized uploads; asked for a non-blocking metric for packer duration. Author replies: added enhanced sanitizer coverage and a private-deploy S3 adapter that uses instance profile/KMS, strengthened RBAC check and added the size enforcement test and histogram metric for packer.duration_ms. Final approval from Samir and Elena after a tiny naming nit.
source.ci_summary: CI run passed: unit tests 312 passed, linters OK, integration smoke job succeeded on linux-amd64 matrix. One flaky test (test_uploader_retry) was quarantined with a follow-up ticket ENG-3348 and excluded from blocking list.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash on 2026-02-20 by Samir Patel, merge commit message summarizes feature and docs. Release notes added to changelog under incident-bot v1.18.0.
source.release_notes: Added quick-check CLI and diagnostic packer: a fast, size-limited diagnostic collector that integrates with incident-bot slash-commands. Opt-in telemetry, private-deploy S3 adapter, and docs/runbooks/quickcheck.md included.
source.notes_for_ops: When rolling to private deployments, ensure S3 adapter has IAM role or KMS access. Default packer size limit is 8MB; adjust for high-fidelity customers via config. We added a metric packer.duration_ms and packer.output_bytes for SLO monitoring.
source.post_merge_tasks: 1) follow-up ENG-3348 to stabilize uploader retries, 2) add opt-in telemetry toggle in console (PM), 3) run a controlled rollout to 10% of oncall rotations for 2 weeks and collect feedback, 4) add Conjur/KMS docs for on-prem S3 adapter.
source.safety_considerations: Packer runs in an ephemeral container by default and does not transmit raw tokens or secrets; sanitizers remove common secrets and the CLI has a dry-run mode to inspect what would be collected. Uploads produce short-lived presigned URLs and default retention is 72 hours. Admins can disable uploads in private deployments.
source.testing_notes: Includes unit tests for sanitizers, size limits and uploader mocks. E2E smoke test exercises slash-command flow against a local instance. Manual test steps in docs/runbooks/quickcheck.md. Recorded performance: median pack duration 220ms in staging for common workloads.
source.related_work: Related to ENG-2901 (incident automation) and the incident-bot effort to reduce mean time to mitigate. Complements the diagnostic snapshotter PRs by producing a compact, attachable file suited for quick triage rather than full forensic bundles.
source.author_signature: Ava Chen - Implemented the packer and CLI, coordinated docs and tests.

source.title: handoff: privacy filter and attachment rate-limit proxy
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.description: Add a configurable PII redaction pipeline to handoff notes and an ephemeral attachment proxy with per-incident and per-user rate limits. Motivation: customers reported accidental PII leakage in handoff notes and large attachments causing spiky egress and long-term retention burdens. This PR implements a layered approach: 1) a lightweight PII classifier + heuristic regex scrubber exposed as internal/handoff/scrubber.go with three redact levels (none, redact-pii, redact-sensitive) and config driven allowlists; 2) an attachment proxy service internal/proxy/attach_proxy.go that accepts multipart uploads, applies size/type heuristics, stores content in an ephemeral blobstore with TTL and returns short access tokens; 3) enforcement of per-incident and per-user upload rate limits via a sliding-window counter stored in Redis with configurable quotas and backoff; 4) telemetry and audit hooks in slo-toolkit for redaction-rates and proxy throttling; 5) docs and runbook updates for oncall handling of throttled uploads. Backwards compatibility: the redaction pipeline is behind feature flag handoff.redaction.enabled=false by default and proxy is optional with handoff.attachments.proxy_url unset by default. Rollout plan: enable in staging for 10% of incidents for 48h, monitor redaction false-positive/false-negative rates and proxy 429 rate, then ramp to 100% if metrics are green. Includes migration to replace legacy long-lived attachment URLs with ephemeral tokens for new uploads only; existing attachments are untouched. Manual rollback: flip feature flags and scale down proxy deployment. Testing: unit tests for scrubber heuristics, integration tests for proxy upload/TTL, e2e handoff smoke tests with redaction enabled. See changed_files for implementation details and a short rollout checklist.
commits: feat(handoff): add scrubber pipeline with redact levels and allowlists
feat(proxy): implement ephemeral attachment proxy and TTL semantics
chore(config): add feature flags and quota knobs to config schema
docs: add handoff privacy guide and operator runbook steps
changed_files: internal/handoff/scrubber.go — new PII classifier + heuristic scrubber and tests
internal/proxy/attach_proxy.go — multipart upload handler, TTL store, token issuer
slo-toolkit/telemetry/redaction_metrics.go — metrics for redacted fields and FDP rate
cmd/incident-bot/main.go — feature flag wiring and proxy client integration
docs/handbook/handoff_privacy.md — operator guide, FAQ, rollout checklist
review_comments: Daniel Kim: Prefer the scrubber be opt-in for existing customers; requested a feature flag and a migration note. PRIYA: Added handoff.redaction.enabled flag and migration section in docs; responded with updated description and windows for canary.

Rosa Martinez: Concern about false positives for code snippets and stack traces. Requested escape hatch and an allowlist for monospaced blocks. PRIYA: Added monoblock allowlist and unit tests demonstrating preserved code blocks when marked.

Ethan Blake: Noted potential race condition in Redis quota decrement on concurrent uploads and asked for idempotency. PRIYA: Switched quota logic to use Redis Lua script for atomic check-and-decrement and added a test to simulate concurrent uploads. Asked reviewer to re-check the Lua script; Ethan approved after run.
Rosa Martinez: Asked to add a short privacy note to release notes describing what gets redacted. PRIYA: Added a concise note and pointed to docs/handbook/handoff_privacy.md. Rosa approved.
ci_checks: CI: unit-tests — initial run failed (scrubber heuristics) — fixed, re-run passed
CI: lint — failed due to formatting in attach_proxy.go — fixed with gofmt commit
CI: integration-tests (proxy) — passed in staging CI with mocked blobstore
CI: e2e (smoke) — passed on canary subset after feature flag enabled
source.release_notes: Operator-facing: added optional handoff privacy scrubber (feature-flagged) and an ephemeral attachment proxy with per-incident and per-user rate limits. Admins can configure redact levels and quota knobs in config. See docs/handbook/handoff_privacy.md for details and rollback instructions.

Testing: unit tests for scrubber heuristics, integration tests for proxy upload/TTL, e2e handoff smoke tests with redaction enabled. See changed_files for implementation details and a short rollout checklist.
text: Add a configurable PII redaction pipeline to handoff notes and an ephemeral attachment proxy with per-incident and per-user rate limits. Motivation: customers reported accidental PII leakage in handoff notes and large attachments causing spiky egress and long-term retention burdens. This PR implements a layered approach: 1) a lightweight PII classifier + heuristic regex scrubber exposed as internal/handoff/scrubber.go with three redact levels (none, redact-pii, redact-sensitive) and config driven allowlists; 2) an attachment proxy service internal/proxy/attach_proxy.go that accepts multipart uploads, applies size/type heuristics, stores content in an ephemeral blobstore with TTL and returns short access tokens; 3) enforcement of per-incident and per-user upload rate limits via a sliding-window counter stored in Redis with configurable quotas and backoff; 4) telemetry and audit hooks in slo-toolkit for redaction-rates and proxy throttling; 5) docs and runbook updates for oncall handling of throttled uploads. Backwards compatibility: the redaction pipeline is behind feature flag handoff.redaction.enabled=false by default and proxy is optional with handoff.attachments.proxy_url unset by default. Rollout plan: enable in staging for 10% of incidents for 48h, monitor redaction false-positive/false-negative rates and proxy 429 rate, then ramp to 100% if metrics are green. Includes migration to replace legacy long-lived attachment URLs with ephemeral tokens for new uploads only; existing attachments are untouched. Manual rollback: flip feature flags and scale down proxy deployment. Testing: unit tests for scrubber heuristics, integration tests for proxy upload/TTL, e2e handoff smoke tests with redaction enabled. See changed_files for implementation details and a short rollout checklist.

source.title: add incident-capture CLI plus ephemeral playbook templates and safe artifact bundler
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Adds an opinionated `incident-capture` CLI that produces safe, redacted incident bundles and a set of ephemeral playbook templates for faster oncall response. Backwards-compatible. No config migration required.
source.description:
Motivation: oncall responders repeatedly need a compact, reproducible snapshot of system state that is safe to share with SRE/eng. Existing runbook generators either produce too much PII or require interactive console steps. This PR introduces a lightweight CLI that: 1) collects a tunable set of diagnostics (logs, system metrics, trace snippets, recent error samples), 2) performs automated redaction rules and entropy checks to avoid shipping secrets, and 3) packages artifacts into an ephemeral bundle with a reproducible hash and a short-lived presigned link for sharing. In addition, we include a small library of ephemeral playbook templates (network-isolation, safe-restart, rolling-back) that are parameterized and renderable by the incident-bot UI.

What changed (high level): added new binary `cmd/incident-capture` with subcommands `collect`, `redact`, `bundle`, `preview`; new Go package `pkg/capture` that encapsulates collectors and redactors; playbook templates under `playbooks/ephemeral` with YAML templates and parameter schema; a lightweight HTML previewer for the bundle summary in `ui/components/BundledSummary`; tests for redaction heuristics; added guard rails that refuse to include full heap dumps unless the caller passes `--include-heap-dump` with an acknowledged flag; added telemetry events (opt-in) and a CLI dry-run mode.

Checklist: - [x] design doc linked in Linear (ENG-2345) - [x] unit tests for redaction rules - [x] e2e smoke collection in CI - [x] safety review by security team - [x] UX review for playbook templates

Backward compatibility & migration: no changes to the incident-bot API surface. New CLI is additive. Existing runbooks continue to work. Operators can enable the presigned link feature via a new feature flag `ephemeralBundles.enabled` (defaults to false in enterprise/private installs).
source.commits:
(1) init: scaffold incident-capture CLI and cmd wiring
(2) feat: add collectors (logs, traces, metrics) and basic redaction engine
(3) feat: bundleer with tar+gzip + metadata and short-lived link generator (stubbed for private installs)
(4) feat: playbook templates and UI summary component
(5) test: add unit + e2e tests and fixture bundles
(6) fix: tighten redaction regexes per security review
source.review_thread: Diego Alvarez: Can we make the default retention for generated presigned links 1 hour instead of 24h? Maya Chen: Good call — changed default to 1h and added feature flag. Priya Natarajan: Redaction regex looks broad; I found two false negatives in tests. Maya Chen: tightened regexes and added test cases for credit-card-like tokens and JWT fragments. Liam O'Connor: UX looks good; can we add a small banner warning when heap dumps are included? Maya Chen: added an explicit confirmation flag and a banner in the previewer. Diego Alvarez: +1 pending CI. Priya Natarajan: Approved. Liam O'Connor: Approved. Maintainer (Sarah Bloom): Squash-merged after CI green and security ack.
source.notes_for_ops: For Private/Enterprise installs, operators must set `ephemeralBundles.enabled=true` and configure signing keys for presigned link generation. The installer package `redwood-private-installer` will expose a new option. Default is disabled to avoid accidental data exfiltration.
source.associations: Related: ENG-2345 (design), SUP-4312 (customer case that motivated this), DOC-1024 (docs update ticket)
source.post_merge_tasks: (1) rollout announcement to SRE channels with usage guidance (2) coordinate with security to run a weekend soak on staging for bundle contents (3) add telemetry dashboards for bundle creation and share rate
source.manual_test_instructions: 1) build: `go build ./cmd/incident-capture` 2) run dry-run: `./incident-capture collect --service=api --since=5m --dry-run` 3) run full bundle (will refuse heap dump unless `--include-heap-dump --ack-heap-dump`): `./incident-capture collect --service=api --since=30m | ./incident-capture redact | ./incident-capture bundle --presign-ttl=1h` 4) open preview: `open out/bundle-summary.html`

source.title: Post error budget burn summaries to #eng-sre on burn-rate alerts (dashboards + runbook links)
source.author: Sergio Costa
source.description:
Context: As part of the tiered SLO + error budget program, we’re standardizing burn-rate alerting and enforcement. Today the burn-rate alerts page the on-call, but triage is still slow because the initial signal doesn’t include (a) how fast we’re burning, (b) what’s driving it (latency vs errors vs streaming disconnects), or (c) where to go next (dashboards/runbooks). This PR updates incident-bot to listen for burn-rate alert notifications and post a structured “burn summary” message into #eng-sre (and thread replies for updates), including deep links to the tiered SLO dashboards and the active mitigation runbook.

What this does: 1) Adds a new alert type handler for error budget burn-rate alerts (Prometheus Alertmanager payloads via our existing webhook). 2) Builds a canonical Slack message format: header with SLO name + tier + region, a short diagnosis (burn rate + window + budget remaining), and a set of links (Grafana dashboard, logs/traces, runbook). 3) Adds a lightweight “top contributors” section when the alert includes a `route` or `endpoint_class` label (to avoid cardinality explosion we only surface what’s already in the alert labels). 4) Dedupe/ratelimit: repeated firings for the same SLO/tier/region within 15 minutes update an existing thread instead of spamming the channel. 5) Safety: scrub potentially sensitive labels (customer/account identifiers) and only allow a safe-label allowlist to render in Slack.

Message contents (example): - Title: “Error budget burning: hosted / us-east-1 / chat.completions (p95 latency)” - Summary line: “Burn rate: 6.2x (2h window), budget remaining (30d): 62% → projected exhaustion in ~4.8d if sustained.” - Links: “Tiered SLO dashboard”, “Regional comparison”, “Runbook: error budget breach mitigation”, “Incident process (when to declare)”. - Footer: build/version and alert fingerprint.

Config: - Adds `BURN_SUMMARY_CHANNEL` (default #eng-sre in prod) and `BURN_SUMMARY_ENABLED` feature flag (default false). - Adds `GRAFANA_BASE_URL` and `GRAFANA_DASHBOARD_UID_TIERED_SLO` for generating direct links with pre-filled variables (tier/region/endpoint_class). - Adds `RUNBOOK_URL_ERROR_BUDGET_BREACH` to keep the link stable as Confluence pages move.

Rollout plan: - Stage: enable in warn-only mode (`BURN_SUMMARY_ENABLED=true`, channel #eng-sre-sandbox) for 1 week; validate formatting + dedupe behavior. - Prod: enable to #eng-sre after signoff from SRE on-call and CS escalation owners. - Follow-up: once stable, add an @-mention for the correct rotation based on tier (Dedicated vs Hosted) and severity mapping.

Out of scope: - Computing burn-rate itself (handled by recording rules + alerts in redwood/observability-pack). - Any new per-customer metrics; we explicitly avoid printing customer/account identifiers.

Refs: - Related alerting work: redwood/pr-28492-add-burn-rate-alerting-queries-and-recording-rules.json - Related dashboards: observability-pack/pr-6312-tiered-slo-dashboards-and-annotations.json - Runbook: confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/error-budget-breach-mitigation-runbook.json
commits: incident-bot: add burn-rate alert handler and Slack message builder
incident-bot: add safe-label allowlist + PII scrubber
incident-bot: add Grafana link generator (tier/region/endpoint_class vars)
incident-bot: add dedupe + thread update logic for repeated firings
tests: unit tests for label scrubbing, link generation, and dedupe key
docs: update README with new env vars + rollout guidance
conversation: Aisha Bello (review): Love the direction. Please make sure we don’t surface any customer identifiers in the Slack message. Some alerts include `account_id`/`customer` labels from downstream exporters; we should scrub by default and only allowlist known-safe labels (tier, region, endpoint_class, slo, window).

Sergio Costa (author): Agreed. Added `internal/sanitize/labels.go` with an explicit allowlist and a denylist of common sensitive keys. Message builder now renders only allowlisted labels; everything else is dropped. Added tests for `account_id`, `customer`, and `org` variants.

Rafael Mendes (review): Can we ensure dedupe doesn’t hide state transitions? If the alert goes from warning → critical, we should post an update (thread reply) with the new severity + updated burn rate.

Sergio Costa (author): Yep. Dedupe key includes `(slo, tier, region, indicator)` but we treat severity change as an update condition and post a thread reply even within the 15m TTL. Added test coverage for severity transition.

Logan Wright (review): Link generation: please use dashboard variables rather than hard-coded query params that might change. Also avoid putting raw route names into URLs if they can be high-cardinality.

Sergio Costa (author): Updated to use Grafana dashboard variables only (`var-tier`, `var-region`, `var-endpoint_class`). Dropped `route` from URL generation entirely; if route exists we only show it in the message when it’s already in the alert labels and passes allowlist (currently it does not).
Aisha Bello (review): One more thing: the runbook link should be a config value, not hard-coded. Confluence URLs tend to move.
Sergio Costa (author): Done: `RUNBOOK_URL_ERROR_BUDGET_BREACH`. README updated with expected values for stage/prod.
Rafael Mendes (approval): Looks good. This should materially speed up triage. Approving with staged rollout per plan.
Logan Wright (approval): Approved. Thanks for keeping the label hygiene tight.
checklist: [x] Links validated against tiered SLO dashboard UID in staging Grafana
[x] Runbook link configurable via env var
source.release_notes: Incident-bot now posts structured error budget burn summaries (with dashboard + runbook links) to #eng-sre when burn-rate alerts fire; includes dedupe/thread updates and label scrubbing to prevent sensitive data leakage.

source.title: Sessioned runbook orchestrator and alert seatbelt
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.description: Context and motivation: On-call teams repeatedly asked for a way to bind ephemeral debugging sessions to runbooks so that responders and secondary engineers can join a single context (links, logs, temporary access) and avoid duplicate work. In parallel we needed a short-term noise-dampening mechanism (an "alert seatbelt") that automatically tempers noisy symptom alerts while keeping SLO-critical signals unsuppressed. This PR implements a sessioned runbook orchestrator plus an SLA-aware alert seatbelt feature.
commits: a1f3b7c: feat(runbook): add session lifecycle and TTL (start/stop/extend)
d2b9e41: feat(integrations): pagerduty session bindings and on-call presence hooks
c7d4f12: feat(alerts): add SLA-aware seatbelt rules and dynamic silence manager
f139a2b: docs/tests: add e2e session flow test and oncall-session.md
review_comments: Samir Chen (review): Can we make session lifecycle operations idempotent? I hit a flake where repeated start events created duplicate records.
Priya Natarajan (author): Good catch — added an idempotency key and dedupe in session_store.go; left note in comments explaining TTL semantics.

Lena Fischer (review): Seatbelt is promising. Please ensure we never silence alerts that are tagged sla:critical unless explicitly allowed by SRE. Also add metrics for 'seatbelt_applied' and 'seatbelt_blocked'.
Priya Natarajan (author): Added a small allowlist for SLA-critical tags and exported two counters: seatbelt_applied_total and seatbelt_blocked_total. Documented in oncall-session.md.
Diego Ramos (review): e2e failed on first run (timeout). Can we make the test resilient to clock skew and transient PD webhook delays?
Priya Natarajan (author): Adjusted waits in the e2e test and added retry backoff for PD webhook simulation. Re-ran CI — green.
Samir Chen (approve): Looks good to me after the idempotency fix and the added metrics. Approving to merge.
Lena Fischer (approve): Docs look sufficient; leaving a minor comment about examples — resolved. Approving.
source.ci_summary: Initial CI: lint=pass, unit=pass, e2e=fail (flaky timeout on session handshake). After adjusting e2e retries and idempotency fix: lint=pass, unit=pass, e2e=pass. All workflows green on rerun.
release_notes: Added sessioned runbook orchestrator: start/stop/extend sessions tied to runbooks with TTLs and audit logs.
Introduced SLA-aware alert seatbelt: temporary noise-dampening that respects sla:critical tags and emits metrics.
PagerDuty and Slack integrations: session bindings and presence notifications to speed handoffs.
New CLI commands: 'incident-bot session start|extend|end' and UI session modal for runbooks.
E2E coverage for session flow and a new docs page: docs/oncall/oncall-session.md
source.notes_for_oncall: When a seatbelt is applied it will create an audit entry and an event in the incident timeline. If you need to escalate, use 'session escalate' which preserves the session context and notifies the next rotation. Seatbelt will not mute alerts with tag 'sla:critical' by default.
source.testing_instructions: Unit tests added for orchestrator concurrency; run 'make test' and 'make e2e-session' to validate flows locally. To simulate PagerDuty webhook locally run 'scripts/pd-sim --session' and observe session lifecycle transitions in logs.
source.security_considerations: Session links are short-lived and stored encrypted in the session_store. Access control enforced via existing RBAC checks. PagerDuty webhook secrets continue to be verified and session extension requires oncall token or explicit approver role.
followup_tasks: ENG-9321: Add session handoff analytics to Console (dashboard card)
SRE-421: Evaluate seatbelt thresholds across teams and tune defaults
ENG-9402: Add retention policy for session artifacts older than 90 days

source.title: Prompt for + pin related SUP tickets and customer-impact summary in incident bridge channels
source.author: Benji Okafor
source.body: Context: Support/SRE asked for a consistent way to keep customer escalations discoverable after a bridge ends. Today, bridge channels frequently miss links to the SUP ticket(s), and the customer-impact summary gets repeated across Slack/Jira/email with drift. This PR updates incident-bot to (1) prompt the bridge creator for related SUP tickets and a customer-impact summary, (2) post and pin a single "Escalation context" message in the bridge channel, and (3) keep the channel topic synced with SUP links (without including customer names). Behavior: - When a bridge channel is created via the bot (or when the bot detects it has been invited to a newly created bridge channel), the bot posts a message with two required actions: "Add related Jira tickets" (accepts SUP-* and optionally INT-*), and "Add customer impact" (free text w/ guidance). - Once submitted, the bot edits the message into a structured summary and pins it. - The bot also sets the channel topic to include: "SUP: SUP-xxxx, SUP-yyyy" plus a short impact tag (e.g., "impact: elevated 5xx, eu-west") and a link to the escalation playbook section on bridges. - The message includes a reminder not to paste customer-sensitive details; it focuses on symptoms/impact/request IDs. - The message provides a one-click "Update context" action to edit tickets/impact later; updates preserve history in a small "edited" footer line with timestamp + editor. Guardrails: - Only allows keys matching ^SUP-\d+$ (and ^INT-\d+$) unless the user explicitly selects "Add other Jira key" (disabled by default). - If no SUP tickets are provided within 10 minutes, the bot posts a follow-up nudge tagging the bridge creator and the current on-call group (configurable per workspace env). - If channel is moved/renamed into #incidents conventions, the pinned message remains and is re-pinned if it was unpinned. Notes: This is intentionally not doing Jira API lookups (permissions vary and we want to avoid leaking summaries into Jira unintentionally); it’s just linking + standardizing context in Slack. Follow-ups: INT-333 will enforce required fields on the Jira side; this PR is the Slack-side piece. Testing: added unit tests for parsing keys, pin idempotency, and topic formatting; added an integration test stub for Slack modal submissions (mocked Slack client).
commits: incident-bot: add bridge "escalation context" prompt on channel creation
incident-bot: validate SUP/INT keys + normalize input
incident-bot: implement pinned message lifecycle (create/edit/re-pin)
incident-bot: sync channel topic with SUP links (no customer names)
tests: add coverage for key parsing, topic formatting, pin idempotency
docs: update runbook snippet for bridge creators
source.ci_summary: CI (GitHub Actions): ✅ lint, ✅ typecheck, ✅ unit tests, ✅ build, ✅ integration (mock slack).
source.review_conversation: Rafael Mendes (request changes, 2026-02-11): "Pinning is great. Two asks: (1) topic should not include account/company names; keep it to SUP keys + generic impact tag; (2) make the pin operation idempotent so retries don’t create multiple pinned messages." Benji Okafor (2026-02-11): "Ack. Updated to remove any free-form customer field from topic and added pin lookup by message metadata so we edit existing instead of posting new." Dylan Brooks (comment, 2026-02-11): "Can we accept INT-* as well? Support often links the internal automation follow-up." Benji Okafor (2026-02-11): "Added optional INT-* support and explicit validation; other keys require a config flag." Owen Phillips (comment, 2026-02-12): "Love the nudge after 10 min. Please make sure it only tags creator + on-call when channel name matches bridge convention." Benji Okafor (2026-02-12): "Done—only triggers for channels created via bot or matching /^bridge-/." Sean Gallagher (approval, 2026-02-12): "Looks aligned with playbook refresh. +1 to merging; will help with post-incident audit trails."
source.release_notes: Incident bridges now include a pinned "Escalation context" message capturing related SUP tickets and a customer-impact summary; channel topics are auto-updated with SUP links for easier cross-referencing.

title: Prompt for + pin related SUP tickets and customer-impact summary in incident bridge channels
preview: Context: Support/SRE asked for a consistent way to keep customer escalations discoverable after a bridge ends. Today, bridge channels frequently miss links to the SUP ticket(s), and the customer-impact summary gets repeated across Slack/Jira/email with drift. This PR updates incident-bot to (1) prompt the bridge creator for related SUP tickets and a customer-impact summary, (2) post and pin a single "Escalation context" message in the bridge channel, and (3) keep the channel topic synced with SUP links (without including customer names). Behavior: - When a bridge channel is created via the bot (or when the bot detects it has been invited to a newly created bridge channel), the bot posts a message with two required actions: "Add related Jira tickets" (accepts SUP-* and optionally INT-*), and "Add customer impact" (free text w/ guidance). - Once submitted, the bot edits the message into a structured summary and pins it. - The bot also sets the channel topic to include: "SUP: SUP-xxxx, SUP-yyyy" plus a short impact tag (e.g., "impact: elevated 5xx, eu-west") and a link to the escalation playbook section on bridges. - The message includes a reminder not to paste customer-sensitive details; it focuses on symptoms/impact/request IDs. - The message provides a one-click "Update context" action to edit tickets/impact later; updates preserve history in a small "edited" footer line with timestamp + editor. Guardrails: - Only allows keys matching ^SUP-\d+$ (and ^INT-\d+$) unless the user explicitly selects "Add other Jira key" (disabled by default). - If no SUP tickets are provided within 10 minutes, the bot posts a follow-up nudge tagging the bridge creator and the current on-call group (configurable per workspace env). - If channel is moved/renamed into #incidents conventions, the pinned message remains and is re-pinned if it was unpinned. Notes: This is intentionally not doing Jira API lookups (permissions vary and we want to avoid leaking summaries into Jira unintentionally); it’s just linking + standardizing context in Slack. Follow-ups: INT-333 will enforce required fields on the Jira side; this PR is the Slack-side piece. Testing: added unit tests for parsing keys, pin idempotency, and topic formatting; added an integration test stub for Slack modal submissions (mocked Slack client).

source.title: Enforce owner/type (and postmortem link for Sev0/Sev1) before incident close
source.author: Devon Price
source.release_notes: Incident closure is now gated on incident_type + subtype and resolved owner. For Sev0/Sev1 incidents, a postmortem link (or placeholder follow-up ticket link) is required before /incident close succeeds. Includes a temporary admin override flag and a feature flag for phased rollout.
source.description:
Context: As part of the Reliability Program (incident taxonomy + ownership mapping), we need incident records to be consistently classifiable and auditable. Today, incidents can be closed without an owner, without incident_type/subtype, and (for high severity) without any postmortem placeholder. This makes reporting incomplete and creates churn for SRE/Support when trying to backfill or follow up after the fact.

What this PR does: 1) Adds closure gating to the /incident close flow (Slack modal + command path). An incident cannot be marked Resolved/Closed unless incident_type and subtype are set (or explicitly set to unknown/other per policy) and owner is present (resolved owner via mapping or manually set owner). 2) For Sev0 and Sev1 only, requires a postmortem link OR a placeholder follow-up ticket link (Linear/Jira) before close. The UI copy is explicit: “postmortem doc link or placeholder ticket (e.g., ENG-xxxx)”. 3) Adds a temporary admin override for edge cases: /incident close --override-gates requires incident.manage_overrides permission. Override usage is logged with actor + reason and emits a metric (incident_bot_close_override_total). This is intended for short-term use during rollout (see rollout plan below).

Why: We’re enforcing the policy introduced in the incident classification rollout: classification is required and cannot be deferred until post-incident. Owner assignment is required to ensure accountability and correct routing for follow-up actions. For Sev0/Sev1, a postmortem link (or at minimum a placeholder ticket) is required so there is a tracked remediation artifact.

Implementation notes: - The gating logic is centralized in CloseGuards.evaluate(incident, actor, requested_state) and used by both the interactive Slack modal and the API endpoint handler (to avoid bypass). - Severity check uses the incident’s normalized severity enum (SEV0/SEV1/SEV2/SEV3). Only SEV0/SEV1 triggers the postmortem requirement. - Postmortem requirement accepts: Confluence URLs, Google Docs URLs, or a ticket key/URL for Linear (ENG-/PM-/DES-) and Jira (SUP-/INT-). The validator is intentionally conservative to reduce false positives; we can expand allowed patterns later. - Owner requirement is satisfied by either resolved_owner_team (from owner mapping config) or manually set owner_team. If both exist and differ, we block close and prompt to resolve mismatch to prevent the “wrong owner on paper” issue observed during early rollout.

Rollout plan: - Feature flag: enforce_close_gates (default ON in staging, OFF in prod). - Once merged, we’ll enable in prod for new incidents only for 1 week (guard checks skip incidents created before the enable timestamp) to reduce disruption. After that, enable for all incidents. - Override is available for 2 weeks after prod enable; SRE will remove permissions after adoption stabilizes.

Testing: - Unit tests for guard evaluation (type/owner/postmortem permutations). - Integration test for Slack modal submission validation (ensures user sees actionable error and the modal remains open). - API tests to ensure close endpoint rejects missing fields even if Slack is bypassed.

Screenshots: Included in the PR (Slack modal: Close Incident -> validation error when missing owner/type; postmortem field appears when severity is Sev0/Sev1).

Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Autopopulate postmortem placeholder ticket for Sev0/Sev1 using Linear API (pending credentials + product decision). - Update docs in redwood-docs to reflect the new close behavior (tracked in ENG-4822 checklist).
commits: incident: add CloseGuards and block close when type/owner missing
slack: update close modal UX + inline validation messaging
validators: add postmortem link / ticket key validation helpers
api: enforce guards in close endpoint (non-Slack path)
metrics: emit override usage + close rejection reasons
tests: unit + integration coverage for guard rules
conversation: Rafael Mendes (review): This is the right direction. Two asks: (1) make sure the API path can’t bypass Slack validation, and (2) add an escape hatch but with audit logging. We will need it during rollout.
Devon Price (author): Added CloseGuards at the API handler and introduced --override-gates behind incident.manage_overrides. Override logs include actor + reason and emits a metric for tracking.

Dylan Brooks (review): The owner mismatch case is good to catch. Can we make the error actionable (show both values and how to fix)? Also please confirm we’re not increasing label cardinality anywhere.
Devon Price (author): Updated the error text to show resolved_owner_team vs owner_team and a suggested command to set the correct owner. Metrics are reason codes (enum) only; no dynamic labels.
Dev Patel (review): From Support perspective: requiring a postmortem link is fine, but we often only have a placeholder ticket at close time. Please make the placeholder acceptable and clear in copy.
Devon Price (author): Done—Sev0/Sev1 accepts a Confluence/Doc URL OR Linear/Jira ticket key/URL. Modal copy now explicitly says “postmortem doc link OR placeholder ticket”.
Elliot Price (review): Minor: README needs a note so on-call doesn’t get surprised. Also add one example of a valid ticket key pattern.
Devon Price (author): Added README section “Closing incidents: required fields” with examples (ENG-4822, INT-801) and a note about the temporary override.
Rafael Mendes (approval): Approved. Please coordinate enablement timing in #eng-sre and make sure override permission is limited to SRE on-call managers.
Dylan Brooks (approval): Approved.

source.title: shifted telemetry compressor and humanized handoff note generator
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description: Motivation: On-call handoffs often arrive with large telemetry attachments and ambiguous next steps. This PR introduces a compact telemetry compressor and a humanized handoff note generator to reduce payload size, remove PII, and provide a cognitive-load estimate and a canary-safe rollback playbook seeded from the collected snapshot. The snapshot collector produces a small 'repro trailer' containing minimal trace slices, top-k logs by error signature, sampled heap/disk metadata and a KV diff bundle. The compressor uses a delta-based rolling window (inspired by rsync-style chunking) plus optional gzip fallback to guarantee size and decompression determinism across platforms. The handoff note generator templates the critical actions, suggested playbook steps, and a short 'operator confidence' metric based on recent similar incidents and successful mitigations. Implementation: adds a new CLI verb incidentctl snapshot gather --compress --seed-playbook and a library package pkg/snapshot with Collector, Compressor and Trailer types. A separate pkg/handoff contains the seeder that turns a trailer into a markdown handoff and an optional canary rollback playbook with explicit verify steps and automated guard rails. Security and privacy: redaction is performed by pkg/sanitize with configurable KMS-backed rules and a strict opt-in flag for payload upload to shared storage. Backwards compatibility: the compressor emits a stable v1 blob header; receivers without decompressor will still get an uncompressed JSON summary. Tests: end-to-end tests simulate 3 incident shapes, compression roundtrip checks, and a small fuzz harness for redaction rules. CI: initial run surfaced a flaky TestHandoffTemplate which was fixed by adding deterministic timestamping in tests. Rationale and impact: expected to reduce average snapshot upload size by 6x for verbose cases, speed up multi-region handoffs, and reduce cognitive overhead for the next responder through structured notes. Commit summary: see commits field. Files changed: see files_changed list. Review: requested tighter PII redaction and explicit opt-in semantics, added audit logs for redaction decisions, added unit tests for compressor edge cases, approved after second iteration. Merge: squash merged to main after CI green and two approvals. Related work: referenced ENG-1423 for handoff UX and SRE-88 for snapshot retention policy. Manual rollout: feature gated behind INCIDENT_SNAPSHOT_V2 and off by default in hosted instance.
commits: collector: initial snapshot collector and trailer format (pkg/snapshot)
compressor: delta-chunk compressor with v1 header and gzip fallback (pkg/snapshot/compress)
handoff: playbook seeder and humanized handoff template + tests (pkg/handoff)
review_thread: Liam O'Connor: Please ensure no sensitive tokens are left in the dumped environment. Add audit logs for redaction decisions.
Maya Chen: Added redaction audit entries and tightened env dump to only include whitelisted keys. Added KMS integration notes.
Priya Natarajan: Tests look good but we need an integration test that runs compressor on a 50MB synthetic payload.
Maya Chen: Added a synthetic payload test and optimized chunk window to avoid pathological memory use.
Diego Alvarez: LGTM after minor doc clarifications about opt-in gating.
release_notes: Added incidentctl snapshot gather command with optional compression and playbook seeding.
Introduced compact 'repro trailer' format v1 to reduce telemetry payloads.
Handoff notes include a suggested canary rollback playbook and an operator confidence estimate.
Feature is behind INCIDENT_SNAPSHOT_V2 feature flag and off by default for hosted customers.

source.title: Add /rollback shortcut command to post rollback UI + runbook links in incident channels
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.release_notes:
- New Slack slash command `/rollback` (alias: `/rw-rollback`) posts one-click links to Optimization Config rollback UI and rollback runbook in incident channels.
- Adds channel safety checks to prevent posting internal-only URLs outside approved incident/oncall channels.
source.body:
Title: Add /rollback shortcut command to post rollback UI + runbook links in incident channels

Context
During recent gamedays + the SUP-18421 escalation, the biggest time sink was not executing rollback itself, but getting responders to the right place quickly (Console rollback view + the canonical runbook) while context switching across tabs. This PR adds a tiny “fast path” in incident-bot: a slash command that posts the correct links and a short checklist directly into the incident channel.

What’s included
- New Slack slash command: `/rollback` (alias `/rw-rollback`).
- Posts a message with:
  - Link to Console Rollback UI (Optimization Config history view with the rollback modal entry point).
  - Link to the runbook: “Rollback Optimization Config”.
  - A reminder of the expected decision criteria (p95/p99 regression + 5xx) and the post-rollback verification checklist.
- Optional args:
  - `--env` (default: prod)
  - `--tenant <tenant_slug>`
  - `--model <model_id>`
  - `--region <region>`
  - `--scope <hosted|dedicated|private>` (default: hosted)
  These args don’t mutate anything; they’re used only to build a more specific Console deep link and to display the blast-radius header in the message.
- Safety/guardrails:
  - Only allowed in channels matching `^#incidents(-.*)?$` or `^#oncall(-.*)?$` (configurable via `INCIDENT_BOT_ALLOWED_CHANNELS_REGEX`).
  - If invoked elsewhere, command responds ephemerally with guidance and does not post any internal links.
  - If `--scope private` is provided, we do NOT link to hosted Console rollback UI; we instead post the private operator guide link and the generic runbook section.
  - Requests are logged (structured) with `request_id`, `slack_user_id`, `channel_id`, and parsed args to simplify debugging (no customer payloads).

Why incident-bot vs Console
This is intentionally not part of the rollback product surface area. It’s an operational shortcut for responders to reduce MTTR during active incidents while the Safe Apply + Rollback feature rolls out more broadly (PM-904).

Implementation notes
- Adds a new handler module `commands/rollback.ts` wired into the existing Slack command router.
- Centralizes link construction in `lib/links.ts` to avoid hardcoding Console hostnames across command handlers.
  - Uses `CONSOLE_BASE_URL` and `DOCS_BASE_URL` env vars.
  - Adds `consoleRollbackUrl({ env, tenant, model, region })` helper (returns a deep link with query params when provided).
- Adds a small args parser with strict allowlist flags (reject unknown flags and return help text).
- Includes unit tests for:
  - Channel allow/deny behavior
  - Arg parsing + help
  - Link construction for hosted/dedicated/private scopes

Screenshots (Slack output)
- Example: `/rollback --tenant northbridge --model llama-3.1-70b --region us-east-1`
  Posts:
  - “Rollback links (prod / hosted) — tenant=northbridge model=llama-3.1-70b region=us-east-1”
  - Button: “Open Rollback UI”
  - Button: “Rollback Runbook”
  - Bullet: “After rollback: verify p95 <= baseline + 5xx back to normal; annotate the incident timeline with config_version.”

Testing
- Unit: `pnpm test` (added coverage around new command router path).
- Lint/typecheck: `pnpm lint && pnpm typecheck`.
- Manual:
  - Verified in #incidents-sandbox: message posts correctly and buttons render.
  - Verified in #random: command responds ephemerally and does not post links.

Rollout
- This is behind Slack app permission already granted to incident-bot; no new scopes required.
- Deploy with next incident-bot release (no migration).

Checklist
- [x] Unit tests added for allowlist and arg parsing
- [x] Verified command behavior in non-allowed channels (ephemeral response)
- [x] Links use env-configured base URLs (no hardcoding)
- [x] Reviewed with SRE for incident channel patterns

Commits
1) Add rollback slash command handler + routing
2) Add link helpers + env config
3) Add tests for command allowlist and link output

Changed areas (high level)
- src/commands/rollback.ts
- src/router/commands.ts
- src/lib/links.ts
- src/lib/args.ts
- test/rollback_command.test.ts
- config/env.example

Review conversation
- Sean Gallagher (requested changes): “Can we ensure we don’t leak internal Console URLs into customer shared channels? Some incident channels are bridged.”
  - Aisha Bello: Added channel regex allowlist + explicit deny behavior (ephemeral-only) and made it configurable via env.
- Hannah Rivera: “The copy should remind responders that rollback is a control-plane action and still needs confirmation in Console; avoid implying this command performs rollback.”
  - Aisha Bello: Updated message header + button labels to say ‘Open Rollback UI’ and added ‘This command does not execute rollback’ note.
- Marcus Lin: “Any authz concerns? At minimum, log invocations for auditability.”
  - Aisha Bello: Added structured log event `incident_bot.command.rollback.invoked` with request_id + slack identifiers. No tokens or sensitive params.

CI
- build: pass
- unit-tests: pass
- lint: pass
- typecheck: pass

Merge notes
Squash merged after approvals. No runtime behavior changes outside Slack command surface. Safe to deploy independently of the control-plane rollback feature flag (PM-904), since this only links to UI/docs.

title: Add /rollback shortcut command to post rollback UI + runbook links in incident channels
preview:
Title: Add /rollback shortcut command to post rollback UI + runbook links in incident channels

Context
During recent gamedays + the SUP-18421 escalation, the biggest time sink was not executing rollback itself, but getting responders to the right place quickly (Console rollback view + the canonical runbook) while context switching across tabs. This PR adds a tiny “fast path” in incident-bot: a slash command that posts the correct links and a short checklist directly into the incident channel.

What’s included
- New Slack slash command: `/rollback` (alias `/rw-rollback`).
- Posts a message with:
  - Link to Console Rollback UI (Optimization Config history view with the rollback modal entry point).
  - Link to the runbook: “Rollback Optimization Config”.
  - A reminder of the expected decision criteria (p95/p99 regression + 5xx) and the post-rollback verification checklist.
- Optional args:
  - `--env` (default: prod)
  - `--tenant <tenant_slug>`
  - `--model <model_id>`
  - `--region <region>`
  - `--scope <hosted|dedicated|private>` (default: hosted)
  These args don’t mutate anything; they’re used only to build a more specific Console deep link and to display the blast-radius header in the message.
- Safety/guardrails:
  - Only allowed in channels matching `^#incidents(-.*)?$` or `^#oncall(-.*)?$` (configurable via `INCIDENT_BOT_ALLOWED_CHANNELS_REGEX`).
  - If invoked elsewhere, command responds ephemerally with guidance and does not post any internal links.
  - If `--scope private` is provided, we do NOT link to hosted Console rollback UI; we instead post the private operator guide link and the generic runbook section.
  - Requests are logged (structured) with `request_id`, `slack_user_id`, `channel_id`, and parsed args to simplify debugging (no customer payloads).

Why incident-bot vs Console
This is intentionally not part of the rollback product surface area. It’s an operational shortcut for responders to reduce MTTR during active incidents while the Safe Apply + Rollback feature rolls out more broadly (PM-904).

Implementation notes
- Adds a new handler module `commands/rollback.ts` wired into the existing Slack command router.
- Centralizes link construction in `lib/links.ts` to avoid hardcoding Console hostnames across command handlers.
  - Uses `CONSOLE_BASE_URL` and `DOCS_BASE_URL` env vars.
  - Adds `consoleRollbackUrl({ env, tenant, model, region })` helper (returns a deep link with query params when provided).
- Adds a small args parser with strict allowlist flags (reject unknown flags and return help text).
- Includes unit tests for:
  - Channel allow/deny behavior
  - Arg parsing + help
  - Link construction for hosted/dedicated/private scopes

Screenshots (Slack output)
- Example: `/rollback --tenant northbridge --model llama-3.1-70b --region us-east-1`
  Posts:
  - “Rollback links (prod / hosted) — tenant=northbridge model=llama-3.1-70b region=us-east-1”
  - Button: “Open Rollback UI”
  - Button: “Rollback Runbook”
  - Bullet: “After rollback: verify p95 <= baseline + 5xx back to normal; annotate the incident timeline with config_version.”

Testing
- Unit: `pnpm test` (added coverage around new command router path).
- Lint/typecheck: `pnpm lint && pnpm typecheck`.
- Manual:
  - Verified in #incidents-sandbox: message posts correctly and buttons render.
  - Verified in #random: command responds ephemerally and does not post links.

Rollout
- This is behind Slack app permission already granted to incident-bot; no new scopes required.
- Deploy with next incident-bot release (no migration).

Checklist
- [x] Unit tests added for allowlist and arg parsing
- [x] Verified command behavior in non-allowed channels (ephemeral response)
- [x] Links use env-configured base URLs (no hardcoding)
- [x] Reviewed with SRE for incident channel patterns

Commits
1) Add rollback slash command handler + routing
2) Add link helpers + env config
3) Add tests for command allowlist and link output

Changed areas (high level)
- src/commands/rollback.ts
- src/router/commands.ts
- src/lib/links.ts
- src/lib/args.ts
- test/rollback_command.test.ts
- config/env.example

Review conversation
- Sean Gallagher (requested changes): “Can we ensure we don’t leak internal Console URLs into customer shared channels? Some incident channels are bridged.”
  - Aisha Bello: Added channel regex allowlist + explicit deny behavior (ephemeral-only) and made it configurable via env.
- Hannah Rivera: “The copy should remind responders that rollback is a control-plane action and still needs confirmation in Console; avoid implying this command performs rollback.”
  - Aisha Bello: Updated message header + button labels to say ‘Open Rollback UI’ and added ‘This command does not execute rollback’ note.
- Marcus Lin: “Any authz concerns? At minimum, log invocations for auditability.”
  - Aisha Bello: Added structured log event `incident_bot.command.rollback.invoked` with request_id + slack identifiers. No tokens or sensitive params.

CI
- build: pass
- unit-tests: pass
- lint: pass
- typecheck: pass

Merge notes
Squash merged after approvals. No runtime behavior changes outside Slack command surface. Safe to deploy independently of the control-plane rollback feature flag (PM-904), since this only links to UI/docs.

source.title: Auto-suggest incident_type from symptoms (5xx/latency/streaming/capacity) with override
source.author: Devon Price
source.description:
Context: As part of the Reliability Program "Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping" we’re making incident_type/subtype required in incident-bot. This PR adds lightweight heuristics to auto-suggest a likely incident type based on the initial symptoms so we reduce time-to-classify during live incidents while keeping the human as the source of truth.

What’s in this PR: 1) Suggestion engine: Adds a new suggestion module that generates a (type, subtype, confidence, rationale) suggestion from the incident creation context. Inputs include: incident-bot symptom selections (5xx spike, p95 latency regression, streaming disconnects/resets, capacity/fleet alerts), any attached alert payload (title + common labels), and a small set of structured hints we already capture (tier, region, affected surface: hosted/dedicated/private). The output is always constrained to the v1 taxonomy allowlist; if we cannot match, we return incident_type=Unknown with confidence=low.

2) UX changes (Slack modal + /incident create): When the modal opens, we compute a suggestion and pre-select the incident_type/subtype dropdowns only if the user hasn’t already picked a value. The UI shows a compact hint line: “Suggested: <type>/<subtype> (confidence: <high|medium|low>) — <rationale>”. A new “Use suggested type” toggle is included for cases where preselecting felt too aggressive during review; by default the dropdown is set but the hint is explicit and the user can change it. Any user selection overrides the suggestion immediately and we keep the final selected value as the incident’s incident_type.

3) Auditability: We store suggestion metadata alongside the incident record: suggested_type, suggested_subtype, suggestion_confidence, suggestion_reason_codes, and a boolean suggested_was_overridden (true if final != suggested). This lets SRE report on suggestion accuracy and helps iterate without changing the taxonomy (aligned with the goal of keeping taxonomy stable/low-cardinality).

4) Guardrails: Suggestions are based on an allowlist of patterns and structured symptom flags, not arbitrary freeform strings. Regex usage on alert titles is intentionally limited and reviewed to avoid brittle behavior and accidental cardinality explosions. If multiple heuristics match, we pick the highest-confidence category and include secondary matches in rationale (without auto-setting contributing factors; those remain manual).

Heuristic rules (v1): - 5xx spike: If symptom flag includes “5xx spike” OR alert title/labels match known gateway error alerts, suggest Availability > Elevated 5xx (confidence high). If error hints include “overload”/“queue depth”/“shed”, bias to Availability > Overload/Load shedding (confidence high). - p95 latency regression: If “p95 latency regression” selected and 5xx is not selected, suggest Latency > p95/p99 regression (confidence medium). If alert mentions “runtime scheduler stall” or “kv cache thrash” (known runtime alert names), suggest Latency > Runtime regression (confidence high). - streaming resets/disconnects: If “streaming disconnects/resets” selected OR alert title matches SSE/WebSocket reset monitors, suggest Streaming > Disconnects/resets (confidence high). - capacity/fleet alerts: If symptom flag includes “capacity event” OR alert matches fleet provisioning/insufficient capacity monitors, suggest Capacity > Fleet shortage (confidence high). - Default: Unknown (confidence low) with a short prompt to pick the closest taxonomy category manually.

Testing: - Unit tests for the suggestion engine with representative inputs (symptom-only, alert-only, mixed). - Snapshot tests for Slack modal rendering to ensure the hint line and override behavior stay stable. - Added a small golden test set pulled from recent incident titles (sanitized) to ensure we don’t regress matching behavior.

Rollout: Feature flag `incident_type_suggestions_enabled` (default on for eng-sre + oncall workspaces, off for other internal workspaces until we confirm no UI surprises). A follow-up will enable globally once we have a week of data and review accuracy/overrides.

Notes/Non-goals: This does not auto-route paging or change ownership mapping (handled by control plane + alert routing work). This does not attempt deep multi-cause classification; it’s a first-pass guess to accelerate humans.

References: ENG-4822 (require incident_type + owner), ENG-4821 (taxonomy definitions), ENG-4823 (pager routing), INT-772 (modal field edge case).
commits: feat(suggest): add incident_type suggestion engine and allowlisted mappings
feat(slack): render suggestion hint + override behavior in incident create modal
feat(storage): persist suggestion metadata and override bit for reporting
test: add unit + snapshot tests for suggestions
chore(flags): gate suggestion engine behind incident_type_suggestions_enabled
conversation: Rafael Mendes (review): The overall direction is good. Two asks: (1) please avoid auto-overwriting a value if the IC already picked a type before selecting symptoms; (2) make sure the suggestion is visible even if we don’t preselect (so people understand why it guessed).
Devon Price (author): Updated modal logic so we only preselect when type/subtype are unset, and we always show the hint line. Also added suggested_was_overridden for reporting.

Allison Grant (review): Concern about regexes on alert titles. Can we constrain matching to a small allowlist of known alerts + specific labels? Also ensure we’re not adding any freeform strings to metrics labels downstream.

Devon Price (author): Agree. Moved matching to `allowlist.ts` with explicit patterns for ~18 known monitors and a few label keys. The suggestion engine returns only taxonomy enums + reason codes (no raw alert titles). Added tests for unknown titles returning Unknown/low.

Sofia Ivanova (review): For latency rules: the runtime-specific ones should prefer Runtime regression only when we see known runtime stall/KV thrash monitors. Otherwise keep it generic latency regression. Also, can you include rationale text that doesn’t over-claim root cause?
Devon Price (author): Adjusted latency rule precedence and updated rationale strings to be symptom-based (e.g. “latency regression detected”) rather than causal (e.g. removed “runtime is broken”).

Dev Patel (review): From Support perspective: great. Request: keep confidence + rationale short enough to paste into customer comms, but don’t assume we’ll share it externally. Also, any chance we can make it easy to override when customer reports mismatch (e.g., streaming vs client SDK)?
Devon Price (author): Rationale is now one line and explicitly internal. Override is just changing dropdown; we also show “You changed the suggested type” so ICs know it was overridden.
Rafael Mendes (review): One more: add a feature flag defaulting on only for oncall workspaces to start. Approve after that.
Devon Price (author): Added `incident_type_suggestions_enabled` and set default workspace allowlist in config. Ready for re-review.
Allison Grant (review): Re-reviewed. This is now appropriately constrained. Approved.
Rafael Mendes (review): Approved. Let’s monitor override rate and iterate.
release_notes: incident-bot now suggests an initial incident_type/subtype based on selected symptoms and attached alerts, with explicit confidence and easy override
suggestion metadata is stored for accuracy/override reporting (no impact to external APIs)

source.title: Post overload-protection activations to #incidents with dashboards + runbook links
source.author: Rafael Mendes
source.release_notes: Incident-bot now posts a structured message to #incidents when hot-route overload protection activates (admission control / shedding), including tier/route group context, reason codes, and quick links to the relevant dashboards + runbook.
source.body:
## Summary
This PR adds a new incident-bot event handler for Hot-route Capacity Protection activation events emitted by routing/gateway. When protection enters an active state (or exits), incident-bot posts a structured Slack message to `#incidents` with:
- route_group + route (when available), region, tier class, and activation type
- primary reason code(s) (quota vs overload) and protection mode (admission control vs shedding)
- current/peak metrics snapshot (shed_rate, 429_rate, p95 latency, queue depth where present)
- links to the SRE dashboard + runbook section
- a dedupe key to avoid paging storms during flappy thresholds

This is intended to reduce time-to-diagnosis during overload events and make it obvious when best-effort is being throttled to preserve enterprise SLOs.

## Context
Hot-route capacity protection is rolling out behind flags (see ENG-4927). During the us-east canary we observed multiple on-call threads asking “are we currently shedding?” and “which runbook section applies?”. A lightweight, deterministic bot post was the fastest way to improve shared situational awareness.

## Behavior
### When we post
- On transition into ACTIVE (per region + route_group + tier_class)
- On transition out of ACTIVE (recovery)
- Optional periodic reminder every 20 minutes while ACTIVE if shed_rate remains above threshold (disabled by default; behind `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_REMINDER_ENABLED`)

### Dedupe / rate limiting
- Dedupe key: `(region, route_group, tier_class, mode)`
- Minimum repeat interval: 10 minutes (configurable)
- If the source emits frequent toggles, we collapse and post an update message (thread reply) instead of new top-level posts.

### Message format (example)
Top-level post:
- Title: `Overload protection ACTIVE: us-east / chat_completions / enterprise` 
- Fields:
  - Mode: `admission_control + shedding`
  - Reason: `OVERLOAD_QUEUE_DEPTH` (plus any secondary reason codes)
  - Shed rate (best-effort): `12.4% (5m)`
  - 429 rate (enterprise): `0.3% (5m)`
  - p95 latency (enterprise): `820ms (5m)`
- Links:
  - Dashboard: `Hot Route Protection / us-east / chat_completions`
  - Runbook: `Runbook: Overload protection and admission control (Diagnose)`
  - Debug endpoint (internal): `Admission decision inspector` (only shown if `INCIDENT_BOT_SHOW_DEBUG_LINK=true`)

Recovery post:
- Title: `Overload protection RECOVERED: us-east / chat_completions / enterprise`
- Includes duration in active state and a small “before/after” snapshot.

## Implementation notes
- New event type: `overload_protection.activation.v1`
- Source payload is treated as untrusted input; all fields are schema-validated and truncated to keep Slack payload small.
- We intentionally avoid embedding high-cardinality identifiers (tenant_id, request_id) in the Slack message. The dashboard link includes filters but not tenant scoping.
- Confluence + dashboard links are generated from a simple mapping keyed by `route_group` and `region`.

## Config
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_POSTS_ENABLED` (default: false)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_CHANNEL` (default: #incidents)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_DEDUPE_MINUTES` (default: 10)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_REMINDER_ENABLED` (default: false)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_SHOW_DEBUG_LINK` (default: false)

## Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - schema validation + truncation
  - dedupe + threading behavior
  - link generation
  - ACTIVE -> ACTIVE updates vs ACTIVE -> RECOVERED transitions
- Manual test against Slack sandbox workspace using the replay CLI.

## Rollout plan
1) Merge behind flag (this PR)
2) Enable only in staging for 48h; validate dedupe + message formatting
3) Canary enable in us-east for protected routes only; verify no spam during expected best-effort shedding
4) Expand to other regions post GA enablement window

## Links
- Linear: ENG-4927

Checklist:
- [x] Includes feature flag default-off
- [x] Includes dedupe protections
- [x] Includes tests

commits: Add overload activation event schema + validator
Implement Slack block builder for overload activation + recovery
Add dedupe store + thread update behavior
Add link mapping for dashboard/runbook by route_group/region
Add unit tests + replay CLI fixture
Wire config flags + defaults
conversation: Allison Grant (review): Please confirm we’re not including tenant identifiers anywhere; dashboard filters are OK but make sure the URL doesn’t carry tenant_id. Also, add a note about cardinality in the code comment so this doesn’t regress later.

Rafael Mendes (author): Confirmed. No tenant_id/request_id in text or URL. Dashboard link is route_group + region only. Added an inline comment in `overloadProtectionBlocks.ts` and a unit test asserting disallowed keys are dropped.
Logan Wright (review): Can we include queue depth/scheduler lag only if present and keep field names consistent with our metrics standard? I worry about people comparing apples to oranges.

Rafael Mendes (author): Good call. I’m now mapping payload fields to our standard display names and only showing queue depth when payload declares `source=runtime_metrics`. Otherwise we omit it and keep the message to shed/429/latency snapshot.
Benji Okafor (review): Dedupe interval set to 10 minutes—does that risk hiding quick flaps that matter? Also, ensure we post recovery even if it’s within the dedupe window.

Rafael Mendes (author): Recovery posts bypass dedupe (always post). For flaps, we thread updates rather than posting new top-level messages; added a small “state changed” indicator + timestamp in thread replies.
Dev Patel (review): Support will use these posts during escalations. Can we include the 429 reason code legend link or at least hint ‘quota vs overload’ explicitly in the message?
Rafael Mendes (author): Added a ‘Reason’ field that distinguishes QUOTA vs OVERLOAD and included a link to the 429 reason code docs in the message footer (internal docs link for now).
CI (status): Initially failed on lint due to unused import in replay CLI; fixed in follow-up commit. All checks green after rerun.
Allison Grant (final): Approved. Please keep `INCIDENT_BOT_SHOW_DEBUG_LINK` default false; we should only surface that after INT-3302 access controls land.
Rafael Mendes (author): Kept default false and gated link rendering behind flag. Merging.

title: Post overload-protection activations to #incidents with dashboards + runbook links
preview:
## Summary
This PR adds a new incident-bot event handler for Hot-route Capacity Protection activation events emitted by routing/gateway. When protection enters an active state (or exits), incident-bot posts a structured Slack message to `#incidents` with:
- route_group + route (when available), region, tier class, and activation type
- primary reason code(s) (quota vs overload) and protection mode (admission control vs shedding)
- current/peak metrics snapshot (shed_rate, 429_rate, p95 latency, queue depth where present)
- links to the SRE dashboard + runbook section
- a dedupe key to avoid paging storms during flappy thresholds

This is intended to reduce time-to-diagnosis during overload events and make it obvious when best-effort is being throttled to preserve enterprise SLOs.

## Context
Hot-route capacity protection is rolling out behind flags (see ENG-4927). During the us-east canary we observed multiple on-call threads asking “are we currently shedding?” and “which runbook section applies?”. A lightweight, deterministic bot post was the fastest way to improve shared situational awareness.

## Behavior
### When we post
- On transition into ACTIVE (per region + route_group + tier_class)
- On transition out of ACTIVE (recovery)
- Optional periodic reminder every 20 minutes while ACTIVE if shed_rate remains above threshold (disabled by default; behind `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_REMINDER_ENABLED`)

### Dedupe / rate limiting
- Dedupe key: `(region, route_group, tier_class, mode)`
- Minimum repeat interval: 10 minutes (configurable)
- If the source emits frequent toggles, we collapse and post an update message (thread reply) instead of new top-level posts.

### Message format (example)
Top-level post:
- Title: `Overload protection ACTIVE: us-east / chat_completions / enterprise` 
- Fields:
  - Mode: `admission_control + shedding`
  - Reason: `OVERLOAD_QUEUE_DEPTH` (plus any secondary reason codes)
  - Shed rate (best-effort): `12.4% (5m)`
  - 429 rate (enterprise): `0.3% (5m)`
  - p95 latency (enterprise): `820ms (5m)`
- Links:
  - Dashboard: `Hot Route Protection / us-east / chat_completions`
  - Runbook: `Runbook: Overload protection and admission control (Diagnose)`
  - Debug endpoint (internal): `Admission decision inspector` (only shown if `INCIDENT_BOT_SHOW_DEBUG_LINK=true`)

Recovery post:
- Title: `Overload protection RECOVERED: us-east / chat_completions / enterprise`
- Includes duration in active state and a small “before/after” snapshot.

## Implementation notes
- New event type: `overload_protection.activation.v1`
- Source payload is treated as untrusted input; all fields are schema-validated and truncated to keep Slack payload small.
- We intentionally avoid embedding high-cardinality identifiers (tenant_id, request_id) in the Slack message. The dashboard link includes filters but not tenant scoping.
- Confluence + dashboard links are generated from a simple mapping keyed by `route_group` and `region`.

## Config
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_POSTS_ENABLED` (default: false)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_CHANNEL` (default: #incidents)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_DEDUPE_MINUTES` (default: 10)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_OVERLOAD_REMINDER_ENABLED` (default: false)
- `INCIDENT_BOT_SHOW_DEBUG_LINK` (default: false)

## Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - schema validation + truncation
  - dedupe + threading behavior
  - link generation
  - ACTIVE -> ACTIVE updates vs ACTIVE -> RECOVERED transitions
- Manual test against Slack sandbox workspace using the replay CLI.

## Rollout plan
1) Merge behind flag (this PR)
2) Enable only in staging for 48h; validate dedupe + message formatting
3) Canary enable in us-east for protected routes only; verify no spam during expected best-effort shedding
4) Expand to other regions post GA enablement window

## Links
- Linear: ENG-4927
- Runbook: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGSRE/Runbook%3A+Overload+protection+and+admission+control
- Dashboard spec: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPLAT/Dashboard+spec%3A+Hot-route+protection
- Related PRs:
  - redwood/pr-27441-add-overload-reason-codes-to-429-responses
  - observability-pack/pr-1832-add-hot-route-protection-dashboards

Checklist:
- [x] Includes feature flag default-off
- [x] Includes dedupe protections
- [x] Includes tests
- [x] No high-cardinality fields in message text
- [x] Docs link points to correct runbook section

source.title: Require incident_type/subtype on create; auto-resolve owner + escalation channel
source.author: Devon Price
source.description:
Context: As part of the Reliability Program “Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping”, we’re making incident_type a required field at incident creation time, and we want the bot to resolve an owner team consistently (single primary owner) to reduce time-to-engage and improve reporting quality.

This PR updates the Slack modal + slash command flow to require incident_type and incident_subtype (from the v1 allowlist), then performs owner resolution (type/subtype -> primary owner + escalation channel) and posts the result into the incident channel. The goal is: (1) classification is required and auditable, (2) owner is consistently derived from a versioned mapping, and (3) the IC gets an immediate “who owns this” + “where to escalate” suggestion.

User-facing changes:
- /incident create now blocks submission until incident_type is selected.
- incident_subtype is required when the type has subtypes; otherwise the field is hidden.
- After incident is created, bot posts a short summary: incident_type/subtype, resolved owner team, escalation channel, and a reminder that IC can override classification if needed.
- If mapping is missing (e.g., new subtype), we fall back to SRE on-call owner and label classification as “needs-mapping”; bot also files a lightweight internal warning event.

Implementation notes:
- Added TaxonomyAllowlist (static JSON in-repo for now) with strict validation + display labels.
- Added OwnerResolver that consults the mapping (fetched via config service; local fallback for dev/test).
- Added “override owner” admin-only action in the incident channel (logs audit event; does not change the canonical mapping).
- Added structured fields to incident record: incident_type, incident_subtype, owner_team, escalation_channel, owner_resolution_source.

Out of scope:
- Auto-suggest incident_type from symptoms (tracked separately).
- Retrofitting historical incidents (backfill job lives in redwood/observability).

Rollout:
- Behind feature flag: incident_taxonomy_required_fields.
- Enabled for eng-sre workspace first; then global after 1 week if no issues.

Checklist:
- [x] Slack modal validation for required fields
- [x] Owner resolution + fallback behavior
- [x] Audit/event logging for overrides
- [x] Unit tests for allowlist parsing + resolver
- [x] E2E test for create flow
- [x] Docs snippet in /help output

Screenshots/GIF: attached in PR (Slack modal with incident_type dropdown + subtype conditional).
commits: a1c2c6b incident-create: add incident_type + subtype fields to payload + storage
c3d9c21 slack-modal: enforce required selection + conditional subtype rendering
7b4a01e owner-resolver: map type/subtype to owner team + escalation channel
9e2f5d8 auditing: log owner overrides + resolution source
d6f17aa tests: resolver unit tests + modal validation coverage
f0b8bb2 docs: update /help and example output
testing: manual: created test incident in #inc-bot-dev; verified required dropdown + resolved owner + escalation channel post
conversation: Rafael Mendes (review): We need to be strict about low-cardinality values. Can we guarantee incident_type/subtype only come from an allowlist, and that freeform is impossible via API? Also please ensure the incident record stores the canonical key, not the display label.

Devon Price (author): Yep. Modal options come from taxonomy_allowlist_v1.json; command handler re-validates keys server-side (rejects unknown). Stored fields are canonical keys (e.g., "capacity_fleet_shortage"), display labels are only for Slack UI.

Allison Grant (review): Please add a hard fallback bucket for unknown mappings that doesn’t explode metrics. Suggest: owner_resolution_source="fallback_sre" and incident_type stays as selected, but add needs_mapping=true (internal only). Also confirm we’re not adding subtype as a metric label anywhere in this repo.

Devon Price (author): Implemented needs_mapping as an internal incident attribute; emitted an internal warning event for SRE tooling but no new metrics labels here. Owner resolution source is one of: config, local_fallback, fallback_sre.

Ethan Park (review): For owner mapping, please confirm cache behavior + failure modes. If config service is down we shouldn’t block incident creation. Also, can you align the mapping schema with the control plane versioning approach (ENG-4825)?

Devon Price (author): Resolver never blocks creation. If mapping fetch fails, we fall back to SRE and note owner_resolution_source. Schema matches the draft fields (type/subtype -> owner_team, escalation_channel, runbook_url optional). We’ll swap client to control-plane-backed endpoint once that’s live; tracking in ENG-4825.

Dev Patel (review): From Support/CS POV, escalation channel names should be stable and non-ambiguous (e.g., #eng-fleet-oncall vs #fleet). Can we enforce format + add a note that CS should not use the override action?

Devon Price (author): Added validation that escalation channels must start with # and match a configured allowlist prefix. Override action is restricted to admin role; updated README operator notes to clarify intended use (IC/SRE only).

Dylan Brooks (review): Small UX request: in the incident summary message, include a one-liner: "If classification is unclear, pick best match and add contributing factors in notes". This will reduce folks choosing “unknown” by default.
Devon Price (author): Added the guidance line + link to the decision tree doc (placeholder URL for now; will update when Confluence page is finalized).

Rafael Mendes (approval): LGTM. Thanks for adding server-side validation + non-blocking fallback. Please coordinate rollout window with SRE on-call so we can watch for modal failures (INT-772 history).
Devon Price (author): Ack; will enable flag in eng-sre workspace Monday 10am PT and monitor #eng-sre + internal error dashboard.
source.release_notes: Incident-bot now requires incident_type/subtype during incident creation and auto-resolves owner team + escalation channel (with safe fallback to SRE if mapping is missing/unavailable).
