source.title: Add perf-canary alert handler (Slack threads, dedupe, runbook links)
source.author: Rafael Mendes
source.body:
Summary: Adds a first-class handler in incident-bot for perf-canary regression alerts. The handler formats alerts into a consistent Slack thread, de-dupes repeated firings, and attaches deep links (Grafana dashboard + Confluence runbook + relevant traces) to speed triage.

Context: The perf-canary service will emit alerts as a webhook payload (model + region + tier + metric + observed vs baseline + confidence + baseline version/runtime tags). Before this PR, incident-bot treated canary payloads as generic webhooks which led to noisy, non-threaded posts and no reliable dedupe key, making it hard to triage during regressions and especially during baseline drift/threshold tuning.

What this PR does: 1) Adds a new webhook route + handler for source=perf-canary (Content-Type: application/json). 2) Builds a stable dedupe key and thread key so repeat evaluations (same model/metric/region/severity) update the same Slack thread instead of spamming the channel. 3) Produces a compact Slack message with: top-line regression summary; baseline vs observed; min-samples/confidence; tags (model_version/runtime_build/kernel_tag/hw_sku); and quick links. 4) Adds a lightweight state store interface for dedupe/threads (Redis-backed in prod; in-memory fake for tests). 5) Adds guardrails: drop oversized payload fields, strip unknown labels, and redact any prompt-like fields (defense-in-depth even though canary is synthetic).

Dedupe/threading behavior: - Dedupe key: perf_canary|<env>|<model_id>|<model_version>|<region>|<tier>|<metric>|<severity>|<baseline_id>|<runtime_build>|<hw_sku>. - Thread key: perf_canary|<env>|<model_id>|<region>|<tier>|<metric>|<severity>. - If a new alert matches an existing thread key within the configured TTL (default 6h), we post a reply in-thread and update the thread header (Slack message update) with the most recent observed values. - If severity escalates (warning -> page), we start/attach to the paging thread and include an explicit "Escalated" note with timestamps.

Links: - Runbook: sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert (hardcoded base + slug; environment-aware). - Grafana: deep link to per-model dashboard with prefilled variables (model, region, tier, hw_sku) and time range anchored around first_seen. - Traces/logs: link templates for Tempo/Loki queries using request_route=perf-canary and model_id.

Testing: - Unit tests cover dedupe key generation, Slack block rendering, and TTL behavior. - Integration test covers "first alert creates thread" + "repeat alert updates thread" + "escalation".

Rollout: - Disabled by default behind flag incident_bot.perf_canary.enabled (default false). - Staging enabled for #eng-runtime-canary; prod enablement tracked in ENG-4878.

Checklist: - [x] Adds handler behind feature flag - [x] Slack formatting aligned with SRE paging hygiene - [x] Dedupe TTL configurable - [x] Runbook + dashboard links included - [x] Tests added - [x] Backward compatible with existing generic webhook handler
commits: incident-bot: add perf-canary webhook router and handler skeleton
slack: add perf-canary block kit renderer (compact + triage links)
dedupe: introduce thread/dedupe store abstraction + redis impl
config: add perf_canary handler config (ttl, channels, enable flag)
tests: add unit tests for dedupe and slack rendering
security: clamp payload size + redact unknown/freeform fields
review_conversation: Allison Grant (review): Dedupe key includes baseline_id + runtime_build + hw_sku which is good, but the thread key currently also includes model_version. If model_version changes during rollout, we'll start a new thread and lose continuity. Can we keep thread key at model_id+region+tier+metric+severity and treat model_version as context in the message?

Rafael Mendes (author): Good catch. I’ll adjust: thread key will not include model_version; we’ll still include model_version in the dedupe key to prevent accidental suppression across versions. Also adding a line in the header showing current model_version.

Sean Gallagher (review): Paging channel selection: for Sev2+ we must route to #eng-oncall and @oncall-runtime (per policy). Right now it’s posting to #eng-runtime for both. Please wire severity routing + ensure the message includes a runbook link at the top.

Rafael Mendes (author): Updated routing logic: warn -> #eng-runtime, page -> #eng-oncall + mention group (configurable). Also moved runbook link into the first block and added "ACK / next steps" bullets.
Logan Wright (review): Telemetry links: please make sure Grafana link variables are URL-escaped; also include env in the link. We had broken links previously when model_id contained "/".
Rafael Mendes (author): Fixed by using url.QueryEscape on all vars and adding env as a required variable. Added tests for IDs with slashes and spaces.

Allison Grant (review): Slack formatting: the blocks are a bit long. Can we collapse the attribution tags (runtime_build/kernel_tag/hw_sku) into a single "Attribution" line and avoid a full JSON dump in the thread?
Rafael Mendes (author): Done. Tags are now a single line and we no longer print raw payload; only a compact summary + link to the stored payload in logs (request_id).
Sean Gallagher (approve): Looks good. Feature flag default-off + TTL config meets requirements. Approving.
Allison Grant (approve): Approved with the thread key change and compact blocks.
source.release_notes: incident-bot now supports perf-canary regression alerts with Slack threading, dedupe, and runbook/dashboard links (behind feature flag).

Dedupe/threading behavior: - Dedupe key: perf_canary|<env>|<model_id>|<model_version>|<region>|<tier>|<metric>|<severity>|<baseline_id>|<runtime_build>|<hw_sku>. - Thread key: perf_canary|<env>|<model_id>|<region>|<tier>|<metric>|<severity>. - If a new alert matches an existing thread key within the configured TTL (default 6h), we post a reply in-thread and update the thread header (Slack message update) with the most recent observed values. - If severity escalates (warning -> page), we start/attach to the paging thread and include an explicit "Escalated" note with timestamps.

Links: - Runbook: sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert (hardcoded base + slug; environment-aware). - Grafana: deep link to per-model dashboard with prefilled variables (model, region, tier, hw_sku) and time range anchored around first_seen. - Traces/logs: link templates for Tempo/Loki queries using request_route=perf-canary and model_id.

Testing: - Unit tests cover dedupe key generation, Slack block rendering, and TTL behavior. - Integration test covers "first alert creates thread" + "repeat alert updates thread" + "escalation".

Rollout: - Disabled by default behind flag incident_bot.perf_canary.enabled (default false). - Staging enabled for #eng-runtime-canary; prod enablement tracked in ENG-4878.

Checklist: - [x] Adds handler behind feature flag - [x] Slack formatting aligned with SRE paging hygiene - [x] Dedupe TTL configurable - [x] Runbook + dashboard links included - [x] Tests added - [x] Backward compatible with existing generic webhook handler

source.title: Support approval step for Sev0/Sev1 status page publishes (optional)
source.author: Devon Price
source.description:
Context: As part of the Status page transparency upgrades initiative, we’ve standardized comms templates and added Comms Lead reminders. One gap that came up repeatedly (Support escalations + game days) is that during Sev0/Sev1 incidents we sometimes publish status updates with wording that Support has to immediately clarify, or we publish a component impact that later turns out to be wrong (especially when impact is limited to a subset of customers/regions). This PR introduces an optional approval step in incident-bot before publishing status page updates for Sev0/Sev1, allowing either (a) the on-call Incident Commander or (b) an on-call Support lead to approve the draft. This is intended to improve accuracy without adding overhead to Sev2+ events.

What’s changing:
- Adds a configurable “publish requires approval” gate that activates only for Sev0/Sev1 incidents and only when the action is “publish to Statuspage” (drafting continues to work without approval).
- Approver can be one of:
  - Incident Commander (IC) (default)
  - Support Lead (optional, enabled via config per environment)
- Approval is time-bound: if no approval within N minutes, bot posts a reminder in #incidents and DM’s the Comms Lead + IC. (Does not auto-publish.)
- Adds an explicit “urgent publish” override for IC only (requires a typed confirmation string) for cases where status page time-to-first-update is at risk and Support is unavailable.
- Audit logs: records who approved, when, and the exact message payload + components/regions at publish time (for later accuracy audits).

Key behavior details:
- Sev2+ incidents: no approval gate; behavior unchanged.
- Sev0/Sev1 incidents:
  - /statuspage draft works as today and posts the draft to the incident channel.
  - /statuspage publish triggers approval gate unless config disables it.
  - Approval can happen via Slack button (“Approve publish”) or slash command (/incident approve statuspage).
  - If Support approval is enabled and Support Lead is assigned, Support Lead can approve; otherwise IC approval is sufficient.
- If the incident has no statuspage components selected, publish is blocked with a clearer error pointing to the component fields (ties into the earlier component validation work).

Why optional (and not mandatory): Not all incidents have active Support coverage in the first minutes, and we don’t want to regress TTFU for Sev0/Sev1. The gate can be enabled per workspace/environment. For now we’ll enable in prod for Sev0/Sev1 only, with the IC urgent override available.

Implementation notes:
- Introduces a small “ApprovalGate” interface with a StatuspagePublishGate implementation.
- Slack interactivity: new block actions for approve/deny; deny requires a short reason which is posted back into the incident channel thread.
- Permissions: only users in roles {incident_commander, support_lead} can approve. Role mapping uses the existing incident roles source (no new directory/service).
- Config:
  - statuspage.publish_approval.enabled: true|false
  - statuspage.publish_approval.severities: ["sev0","sev1"]
  - statuspage.publish_approval.approvers: ["ic","support_lead"]
  - statuspage.publish_approval.reminder_minutes: integer

Changed areas/files (high level):
- src/workflows/statuspage/publish.ts: wrap publish action with approval gate
- src/workflows/approvals/gates/statuspage_publish_gate.ts: new gate
- src/slack/blocks/statuspage_publish_approval.ts: Slack UI blocks + actions
- src/slack/handlers/actions.ts: wire approve/deny actions
- src/audit/audit_log.ts: append approval metadata fields
- config/schema.ts + config/defaults.ts: new config entries
- docs/runbooks/statuspage.md: operator guidance for Comms Lead + IC
- tests/workflows/statuspage_publish_approval.test.ts: new coverage for gating + override

Screenshots/UX:
- Added a minimal approval card in the incident channel thread with buttons: Approve publish / Deny (reason required) / IC urgent override.

Rollout plan:
1) Deploy behind config flag off in staging.
2) Enable in staging for Sev0/Sev1 and run through game-day checklist.
3) Enable in prod for Sev0/Sev1 with approvers=[ic,support_lead] and reminder_minutes=5.
4) After 2 weeks, review metrics: time-to-first-update, number of denied publishes, and support escalations about inaccurate status updates.

Risk/mitigations:
- Risk: delays publishing. Mitigation: gate only Sev0/Sev1, reminders at 5m, IC urgent override.
- Risk: role assignment wrong -> no approver. Mitigation: falls back to IC if Support Lead not present; explicit error includes command to assign roles.

Release notes:
- incident-bot: Optional approval step for status page publish actions during Sev0/Sev1 incidents; adds audit logging of approver + timestamps.

Refs:
- Linear: ENG-4860 (approval workflow), ENG-4822 (statuspage integration)
- Jira: INT-10430 (support macros alignment), SUP-28436 (disputed accuracy during US-East degradation)

Checklist:
- [x] Unit tests for approval gate + override
- [x] Updated runbook docs
- [x] Verified no behavior change for Sev2+
- [x] Added audit log fields and verified in staging
commits: incident-bot: add statuspage publish approval gate for sev0/sev1
slack: add approve/deny UI + handlers for statuspage publish approval
config: add publish_approval settings + schema validation
audit: log approval metadata and denial reasons
tests: cover approval flow, timeout reminders, and IC override
docs: update statuspage publish runbook with approval step
review_conversation:
Rafael Mendes: Can we ensure this doesn’t block the very first Investigating update? We’ve got TTFU targets.
Devon Price (author): Yep—drafting is unchanged and can be posted immediately. The gate only triggers on the publish action to Statuspage. Also added IC urgent override with explicit confirmation to avoid accidental bypass.

Dev Patel: Support Lead approval should be optional; sometimes we don’t have someone available in the first minutes. Also please require a denial reason so we can track patterns.
Devon Price (author): Done. Config now controls approvers=[ic] vs [ic,support_lead]. Deny action prompts for a short reason and logs it to audit + posts it in-thread.

Wei Chen: Copy nit: replace “customer impact” with “impact” (we shouldn’t imply we know it’s customer-wide early). Also remove “we believe” from the approval card header.
Devon Price (author): Updated wording in the approval card + related prompts.

Christine Wong: Can the audit log include the component ids/regions that were published? This will help the weekly accuracy audit job.
Devon Price (author): Added component_ids + regions + incident_id + statuspage_incident_id to the audit payload; tests updated.
source.post_merge_notes: Follow-up: enable config flag in prod after next incident-bot deploy window; add a short training note to Comms Lead rotation doc (handled in Confluence/enablement track).

source.title: Statuspage API client: retry/backoff + idempotency keys + audit log entries
source.author: Devon Price
source.release_notes: Improves reliability of automated Statuspage updates by adding retries/backoff, idempotency keys, and an internal audit trail for publish/update actions.
source.description: Problem: incident-bot’s Statuspage integration currently performs best-effort POST/PATCH calls with minimal retry handling. During Sev0/Sev1s we’ve seen transient 5xx/timeout/429 responses (plus occasional network blips) leading to (a) failed publishes that require manual retry and (b) duplicate updates when a human re-triggers a step while the original request is still in flight. This PR introduces a small Statuspage API client wrapper with standardized retry/backoff, idempotency keys, and structured audit log entries for every attempted publish/update. Scope: add an HTTP client with (1) bounded retries for retryable failures (429, 5xx, network timeouts), (2) exponential backoff with jitter and respect for Retry-After where present, (3) deterministic idempotency keys so retries and replays don’t create duplicate incidents/updates, and (4) audit logging to our internal incident-bot audit sink so we can answer “what did we publish, when, and who/what triggered it?” without scraping Slack. Non-goals: changing the copy/templates (handled in PR-19344) or expanding component taxonomy fields (handled in PR-19321). Implementation details: The new StatuspageClient wraps fetch with a retry policy: max_attempts=5 (1 initial + 4 retries), base_delay_ms=250, max_delay_ms=5000, full jitter. We retry on: HTTP 429, HTTP 500/502/503/504, and network errors/timeouts. We do NOT retry on other 4xx. For 429 we honor Retry-After (seconds) if present, otherwise use computed backoff. Idempotency: we add an Idempotency-Key header on requests that create resources (incident create, incident update create). Key format is stable and derived from: statuspage_page_id, incident_id (our internal incident GUID), operation (create_incident | post_update | set_component_status), component_ids (sorted), and a normalized “update_fingerprint” (state + message hash + impact window start rounded to minute). This keeps keys stable across retries but different across meaningfully different updates. Audit log: every call to StatuspageClient emits an AuditEvent with request_id, operation, page_id, statuspage_incident_id (if known), idempotency_key, http_status, attempt_count, elapsed_ms, and redacted payload summary (we keep message length + sha256, not the full message). Audit events go to the existing incident-bot audit stream (and show up in the internal dashboard), and we also log a one-line summary to #incidents-bot-debug on failures after max attempts. Testing: added unit tests for retry classification, backoff calculations (including Retry-After), idempotency key stability, and audit event emission; added one integration-ish test that stubs the Statuspage API with a 429 then a 200 to validate behavior. Ops notes: config defaults live in statuspage_client.yaml but can be overridden via env vars for emergency tuning (MAX_ATTEMPTS, MAX_DELAY_MS).
commits: statuspage: introduce StatuspageClient wrapper with typed operations
statuspage: implement retry policy (429/5xx/timeouts) with backoff + jitter
statuspage: add idempotency key builder + header wiring
audit: add Statuspage publish/update audit events (redacted payload summary)
tests: retry classification + Retry-After handling + idempotency stability
docs: runbook notes on audit trail + debugging failed publishes
review_conversation: Miles Thornton (review): Retry logic looks good. Please confirm we do not retry 400/401/403/404, and ensure we only retry 429 + 5xx + network/timeouts. Also, can we cap total time spent retrying so we don’t stall incident workflows indefinitely?
Devon Price (author): Yep — only 429/5xx/timeouts. I’ll add an explicit predicate + tests for non-retryable 4xx and add a max elapsed cap (default 30s) in addition to max attempts.

Christine Wong (review): Idempotency key includes message hash + state which is great. One concern: impact window rounding to minute could cause collisions if we post multiple distinct updates within a minute with the same template message. Can we include the incident-bot timeline event id (or workflow step id) instead?

Devon Price (author): Good catch. I switched to include our internal incident_event_id when available (workflow has it); if missing, we fall back to the minute-rounded timestamp. Added a test that consecutive updates in same minute generate distinct keys when event ids differ.

Rafael Mendes (review): For audit logs, please confirm we never store the full customer-facing message. Legal asked us to avoid retaining incident copy beyond what’s already public unless needed. Message sha + length is fine. Also add page_id + statuspage_incident_id to the audit record so we can correlate quickly.
Devon Price (author): Confirmed — we only store sha256 + length, plus operation metadata. Added page_id and statuspage_incident_id fields (nullable until we get it back from create).
Allison Grant (review): Can we emit a metric for retry count / final failure so we can alert on Statuspage integration degradation? Even just a counter tagged by operation + http_status.

Devon Price (author): Added counters: statuspage_client_requests_total and statuspage_client_retries_total, plus statuspage_client_failures_total. Tags: operation, final_status (or network_error), page_id. Wired into existing metrics sink.
Miles Thornton (review): Looks solid now. Approving once the max elapsed cap lands.
Devon Price (author): Pushed follow-up commit adding max_elapsed_ms=30000 default + env override + tests. Please take another look.
Miles Thornton (review): Approved.
Christine Wong (review): Approved.
Rafael Mendes (review): Approved.
Allison Grant (review): Approved.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash into main by Rafael Mendes. Squash commit message: "Statuspage API client: retry/backoff + idempotency keys + audit log entries"

Audit log: every call to StatuspageClient emits an AuditEvent with request_id, operation, page_id, statuspage_incident_id (if known), idempotency_key, http_status, attempt_count, elapsed_ms, and redacted payload summary (we keep message length + sha256, not the full message). Audit events go to the existing incident-bot audit stream (and show up in the internal dashboard), and we also log a one-line summary to #incidents-bot-debug on failures after max attempts. Testing: added unit tests for retry classification, backoff calculations (including Retry-After), idempotency key stability, and audit event emission; added one integration-ish test that stubs the Statuspage API with a 429 then a 200 to validate behavior. Ops notes: config defaults live in statuspage_client.yaml but can be overridden via env vars for emergency tuning (MAX_ATTEMPTS, MAX_DELAY_MS).
text: Problem: incident-bot’s Statuspage integration currently performs best-effort POST/PATCH calls with minimal retry handling. During Sev0/Sev1s we’ve seen transient 5xx/timeout/429 responses (plus occasional network blips) leading to (a) failed publishes that require manual retry and (b) duplicate updates when a human re-triggers a step while the original request is still in flight. This PR introduces a small Statuspage API client wrapper with standardized retry/backoff, idempotency keys, and structured audit log entries for every attempted publish/update. Scope: add an HTTP client with (1) bounded retries for retryable failures (429, 5xx, network timeouts), (2) exponential backoff with jitter and respect for Retry-After where present, (3) deterministic idempotency keys so retries and replays don’t create duplicate incidents/updates, and (4) audit logging to our internal incident-bot audit sink so we can answer “what did we publish, when, and who/what triggered it?” without scraping Slack. Non-goals: changing the copy/templates (handled in PR-19344) or expanding component taxonomy fields (handled in PR-19321). Implementation details: The new StatuspageClient wraps fetch with a retry policy: max_attempts=5 (1 initial + 4 retries), base_delay_ms=250, max_delay_ms=5000, full jitter. We retry on: HTTP 429, HTTP 500/502/503/504, and network errors/timeouts. We do NOT retry on other 4xx. For 429 we honor Retry-After (seconds) if present, otherwise use computed backoff. Idempotency: we add an Idempotency-Key header on requests that create resources (incident create, incident update create). Key format is stable and derived from: statuspage_page_id, incident_id (our internal incident GUID), operation (create_incident | post_update | set_component_status), component_ids (sorted), and a normalized “update_fingerprint” (state + message hash + impact window start rounded to minute). This keeps keys stable across retries but different across meaningfully different updates. Audit log: every call to StatuspageClient emits an AuditEvent with request_id, operation, page_id, statuspage_incident_id (if known), idempotency_key, http_status, attempt_count, elapsed_ms, and redacted payload summary (we keep message length + sha256, not the full message). Audit events go to the existing incident-bot audit stream (and show up in the internal dashboard), and we also log a one-line summary to #incidents-bot-debug on failures after max attempts. Testing: added unit tests for retry classification, backoff calculations (including Retry-After), idempotency key stability, and audit event emission; added one integration-ish test that stubs the Statuspage API with a 429 then a 200 to validate behavior. Ops notes: config defaults live in statuspage_client.yaml but can be overridden via env vars for emergency tuning (MAX_ATTEMPTS, MAX_DELAY_MS).

source.title: Generate Statuspage update drafts from incident state (phase templates + metrics)
source.author: Devon Price
source.description:
Context: As part of the status page transparency upgrades, we want to reduce time-to-first-update and improve consistency across incident phases without increasing on-call burden. This PR adds a draft generator that produces templated Statuspage update text based on the current incident state (Investigating/Identified/Mitigating/Monitoring/Resolved). Drafts pull in the structured fields we already collect at intake (component/region/capability) plus optional impact metrics and known mitigations. The output is a DRAFT only (explicitly labeled) and requires a human publish step.

What this does: Adds a template registry for each incident phase with a small variable set (component_display, regions, capabilities, started_at, current_state, impact_summary, error_rate, p95_latency_ms, affected_routes, customer_visible_symptoms, mitigations_in_progress, fallback_active, next_update_eta). Adds a DraftStatusUpdate action to the incident-bot workflow that (1) reads incident state, (2) computes an impact summary string, (3) renders the appropriate template, and (4) posts the draft to #incidents and stores it on the incident record for copy/paste or for the Statuspage API client step in a follow-up PR. If metrics are missing, the template degrades gracefully (no "N/A" leakage; it omits the line). Includes tone guardrails: do not mention specific customers, do not imply SLA credits, do not speculate root cause, and keep updates < 900 chars for Statuspage readability.

Template examples (rendered): Investigating: "We are investigating reports of elevated errors for Hosted API > Chat Completions in us-east. Some requests may fail or see increased latency. Next update in 30 minutes." Identified: "We identified an issue impacting Streaming responses in eu-west. We are working on mitigation and will provide updates as progress is made." Mitigating: "Mitigation is in progress (traffic shifting + capacity rebalance). Impact is improving but some errors may persist." Monitoring: "Mitigation has been applied and we are monitoring recovery metrics." Resolved: "This incident has been resolved. A post-incident summary will be published when available."

Implementation notes: Templates live in src/comms/templates/statuspage.ts and are keyed by IncidentPhase. We added a lightweight renderer (no external templating deps) to keep the bot footprint small and deterministic. Metric selection is intentionally conservative: error rate is preferred; latency-only degradations are included only when explicitly marked in incident state (avoids false positives). We also include an optional "Next update" line; default is 30m for Sev0/Sev1, 60m otherwise, but can be overridden.

Testing: Unit tests cover template rendering, omission rules, and safety checks (no customer names / ticket IDs). Added snapshot tests for each phase with representative incident payloads (Hosted API, Streaming, Embeddings). Manual test instructions included in docs/runbooks/statuspage-drafts.md.

Follow-ups (not in this PR): wiring to the Statuspage API client wrapper with audit logs (PR-19405) and adding an approval step for Sev0/Sev1 publish actions (PR-19433).

Screenshots/logs: Included in the conversation thread as bot output in a test incident (see "Draft generated" comment).
commits: incident-state: add optional impact metrics + mitigation fields
comms: introduce statuspage template registry by phase
incident-bot: add DraftStatusUpdate workflow step + Slack draft posting
tests: snapshots for phase drafts + redaction/length guardrails
docs: add runbook snippet for generating drafts and editing safely
changed_files: src/incident/state.ts (extend IncidentState with impact/mitigation fields + validation)
source.conversation:
2025-11-13 Devon Price (author): Adds template-based draft generation for Statuspage updates keyed by incident phase. Drafts are posted to #incidents and stored on the incident record; not published automatically.

2025-11-14 Christine Wong: This is the right direction. Two requests: (1) please ensure the Investigating template doesn’t claim "elevated errors" unless we have an error signal (latency-only should say "degraded performance"), and (2) add an explicit "Draft" prefix so nobody copy/pastes without a quick sanity check.

2025-11-14 Devon Price (author): Good call. I added a signal selector: if error_rate present -> "elevated errors"; if only latency flag -> "degraded performance"; otherwise -> "reports of issues". Also added "Draft:" prefix in Slack and an internal "requires_review" marker.

2025-11-15 Wei Chen: Copy feedback: avoid "some requests may fail" for partial regional issues unless we mention the region in the same sentence. Also keep the "Next update" line consistent across phases (Investigating/Identified/Mitigating) and omit it for Monitoring/Resolved.

2025-11-15 Devon Price (author): Updated templates accordingly. Next update now only shows for Investigating/Identified/Mitigating and includes region inline. Monitoring/Resolved omit the line.

2025-11-16 Miles Thornton: Please cap output length (Statuspage truncation is painful). Also ensure we never include internal service names (gateway/scheduler/etc). Prefer the public component display name + region.

2025-11-16 Devon Price (author): Added a 900-char cap with a deterministic truncation strategy (drops affected_routes first, then metric lines, then extra symptoms; never truncates the first sentence). Also added redaction checks to prevent internal service tokens.

2025-11-17 Rafael Mendes: Approved overall. One more: default next_update_eta should follow severity targets (Sev0/1 30m, Sev2 60m, Sev3 120m) and be overrideable from the incident record.

2025-11-17 Devon Price (author): Implemented severity-based defaults + override. Added tests.

2025-11-18 Christine Wong: Re-ran a manual flow on a test incident; drafts look good and align with DES-245. Approving.

2025-11-18 Devon Price (author): Squashed and merged. Follow-up PRs will hook into the Statuspage API client + approval workflow.
source.release_notes: Incident-bot can now generate phase-specific draft Statuspage updates from incident state, including optional impact metrics and mitigation context, with safety/length guardrails.

source.title: Announce circuit breaker state transitions to #incidents and #eng-oncall
source.author: Christine Wong
source.release_notes:
- incident-bot posts circuit breaker OPEN/HALF_OPEN/CLOSED transitions with dashboard/runbook links
- messages include recommended actions and suppression to reduce alert spam
source.description:
Context
As part of the Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding program (ENG-4821), we need a low-latency, human-visible signal when breakers start shedding (OPEN) and when they recover (HALF_OPEN/CLOSED). The current workflow relies on dashboards/alerts only; during incidents this increases time-to-diagnosis and contributes to duplicate triage.

This PR teaches incident-bot to announce breaker state transitions into Slack (#incidents and #eng-oncall) with:
- Transition summary (CLOSED → OPEN, OPEN → HALF_OPEN, HALF_OPEN → CLOSED)
- Scope (region, route, model selector, tenant/tier when available)
- Top contributing signal(s) (queue depth, token/sec saturation, dependency health)
- Links to dashboards and the oncall runbook
- A short “recommended actions” block aligned with the runbook

Behavior
- Posts OPEN transitions to both #incidents and #eng-oncall.
- Posts recovery transitions (OPEN→HALF_OPEN, HALF_OPEN→CLOSED) to #eng-oncall only by default to reduce noise.
- Dedupes repeated OPEN transitions for the same breaker_key within a configurable window (default 10m).
- If event payload does not include route/model details, message still posts with breaker_key + region and a note about missing dimensions.

Implementation details
- Adds a new event consumer for `overload.breaker_state_changed` from the internal event bus (same transport as alert notifications).
- Introduces `BreakerKey` normalization to keep dedupe stable (region + route_group + model_selector + tier + policy_id).
- Adds Slack message formatting using Block Kit, including:
  - severity coloring (OPEN = danger, HALF_OPEN = warning, CLOSED = good)
  - buttons: “Open dashboard”, “Open runbook”, “Search logs”
- Uses `dashboards.redwood.ai` and `runbooks.redwood.ai` deep links with query params prefilled (region, time range last 30m, breaker_key).

Recommended actions (message content)
- OPEN: Verify whether this is capacity saturation vs downstream brownout; check shed-rate + runtime queue depth; confirm Retry-After is being set; page routing owner if fallback loops suspected.
- HALF_OPEN: Watch error budget burn + shed rate; do not raise caps until p95 stabilizes.
- CLOSED: Capture incident timestamp window; confirm alerts cleared; annotate postmortem with breaker_key and top signals.

Safety/guardrails
- Hard cap of 1 Slack message per breaker_key per channel per 10 minutes (configurable).
- Suppresses messages for canary-only breakers unless `announce_canary=true` is set (default false).
- Sanitizes tenant IDs: only posts tenant slug if explicitly marked as `shareable=true` in the event payload.

Testing
- Unit tests for normalization, dedupe window, channel routing, and link generation.
- Integration-style test with a mocked Slack client to validate Block Kit payload.

Rollout
- Deploy behind flag `INCIDENT_BOT_BREAKER_ANNOUNCEMENTS=enabled`.
- Start enabled in staging; prod enablement to follow canary rollout go/no-go email thread.

Screenshots (example Slack message)
[OPEN] Hosted breaker opened: us-east / chat.completions / model=rw-llama-3-70b (tier=self-serve)
Signals: runtime.queue_depth_p95=182, token_saturation=0.93
Shed rate: 14% (last 5m)
Actions: Check Overload Protection dashboard; consider temporary max_tokens cap policy; verify Retry-After present; evaluate fallback policy health
Buttons: Open dashboard | Open runbook | Search logs

Notes
- This PR does not change alerting rules; it is a complementary “human paging context” path.
- Follow-up: thread-safe in-memory dedupe should move to Redis if we run multiple incident-bot replicas (tracked in ENG-4869).
commits: Add breaker state change event types + consumer wiring
Normalize breaker key and implement per-channel dedupe
Add Slack Block Kit message template + dashboard/runbook links
Add tests for dedupe, formatting, and channel routing
Docs: config flags and staging run instructions
source.ci_summary: Buildkite: ✅ build (linux/amd64), ✅ unit tests, ✅ lint, ✅ govet, ✅ integration (mock slack). One flaky integration job on 2025-11-17 reran green.
review_conversation: Rafael Mendes: Can we avoid posting recoveries to #incidents? That channel gets very noisy during multi-signal events.
Christine Wong (author): Yep — switched default so only OPEN goes to #incidents; recoveries stay in #eng-oncall unless `announce_recoveries_to_incidents=true` is set.
Aisha Bello: Links should land on the new overload dashboard, not the generic runtime overview. Also time range should be 30m aligned with our oncall doc.
Christine Wong (author): Updated `dashboards.go` to point at Overload Protection dashboard uid=overload-protection and default range=now-30m..now. Added a test to assert query params.
Logan Wright: Dedupe currently uses process memory; incident-bot is single replica today but we’ve talked about scaling it. Add a comment + follow-up issue so we don’t forget.
Christine Wong (author): Added a TODO in `dedupe.go` and filed ENG-4869 for Redis-backed dedupe if/when we go multi-replica.
Peter Holtz: Please ensure we don’t leak enterprise tenant identifiers into Slack. What’s the source of `tenant_slug`?

Christine Wong (author): Event payload now includes `tenant.shareable` boolean from control plane; we only include slug if shareable=true. Otherwise the message shows `tenant=redacted`. Added test coverage.
Rafael Mendes: One more thing: for OPEN transitions, include a single-line hint about Retry-After to prevent retry storms.
Christine Wong (author): Added to the recommended actions block and linked to the runbook section anchor `#retry-after-and-client-behavior`.

source.title: Incident intake: add required status page component/region/capability fields + validation
source.author: Devon Price
source.description:
Why: We keep losing time (and occasionally accuracy) in the first 10–15 minutes of an incident because the intake form doesn’t capture the info required to pick the right public status page component(s). This led to coarse updates ("API") even when the impact was limited to Streaming or Embeddings in a single region. This PR updates the incident intake workflow to require three customer-facing classification fields (component, region, capability) and adds validation + reminders so Comms Lead can publish component-accurate updates quickly.

What changed (high level): - Adds new required intake fields: status_component_id, impacted_region, impacted_capability - Adds opinionated defaults + "Unknown/TBD" escape hatch to avoid blocking declaration - Adds server-side validation for required fields on incident creation + updates - Writes selected values into the incident record (used later by statuspage integration + summary generator) - Adds lightweight guardrails: if severity >= Sev2 and customer-facing, require the fields; if internal-only, allow empty but warn - Normalizes naming (component_id) to align with status component registry schema being added in observability-pack

User-facing behavior (Slack modal): - New section: "Customer-facing impact" - Component: single-select list (display names) mapping to status_component_id - Region: single-select (us-east, us-west, eu-west, ap-southeast, global) - Capability: single-select (chat, streaming, embeddings, reranking, console, dedicated-control-plane) - Includes helper text + link to component taxonomy doc

Validation rules: - For Sev0/Sev1/Sev2 incidents with "Customer-facing" = true: component + region + capability must be set (can be set to "unknown" values on first pass) - For Sev3+ or "Customer-facing" = false: fields optional, but modal shows a non-blocking warning when empty - On update: disallow clearing these fields for customer-facing incidents (prevents accidental regressions during handoffs)

Rollout notes: - Landed behind feature flag incident_intake.require_status_classification (default off) - Once enabled: the workflow still allows a fast declaration via the "Unknown/TBD" options; bot posts a reminder at +15m if unknown values remain for a customer-facing incident - This PR does not publish to Statuspage; it only captures structured fields (publishing comes in PR-19344)

Testing: - Unit tests for schema validation + backward compatibility for existing incidents without these fields - Manual: ran /incident declare in #incidents-sandbox and verified modal behavior across Sev1 vs Sev3 and customer-facing toggle

Screenshots: (internal) Slack modal before/after in the PR attachments

Related: - ENG-4821 (taxonomy) - ENG-4822 (statuspage integration) - Follow-up PR will backfill these fields on active incidents where possible
commits: incident-intake: add status component/region/capability fields to incident schema
slack modal: render customer-facing impact section + helper text
validation: enforce required fields for customer-facing sev0-sev2 + prevent clearing
feature flag: gate requirement + add reminder when fields remain unknown
tests: schema validation + migration coverage
review_conversation: Rafael Mendes (review): Can we avoid blocking the IC when we don’t yet know the exact component? (We still want fast declaration.)

Devon Price (author): Added "Unknown/TBD" options for each field + a 15m reminder to tighten classification once we have signal. Validation now accepts unknown values but still requires an explicit selection for customer-facing Sev0–Sev2 so we don’t silently omit it.
Allison Grant (review): Please ensure region naming matches what dashboards/alerts will use (us-east vs use1).

Devon Price (author): Updated options to the canonical short names (us-east/us-west/eu-west/ap-southeast/global). Also normalized storage field to impacted_region (string) and added mapping helper for future ingestion from metrics tags.
Christine Wong (review): Do we need multi-select for components? Some incidents span Streaming + Chat.

Devon Price (author): Discussed offline; keeping single component for now with capability capturing the primary customer-visible symptom. Added guidance in helper text: pick the most impacted component for first update; we can extend to multi-component once PR-19344 is in place and we’ve validated Statuspage constraints.
Dev Patel (review): Any risk Support gets contradictory fields vs what we tell customers?

Devon Price (author): Fields are internal-only until publishing. The goal is to align what Comms Lead posts with the taxonomy. Support macros will reference the same component display names once PR-19433 lands.
Rafael Mendes (approval): LGTM with the unknown/TBD path + feature flag. Please coordinate enablement plan in #eng-sre.
Christine Wong (approval): LGTM.

title: Incident intake: add required status page component/region/capability fields + validation
preview:
Why: We keep losing time (and occasionally accuracy) in the first 10–15 minutes of an incident because the intake form doesn’t capture the info required to pick the right public status page component(s). This led to coarse updates ("API") even when the impact was limited to Streaming or Embeddings in a single region. This PR updates the incident intake workflow to require three customer-facing classification fields (component, region, capability) and adds validation + reminders so Comms Lead can publish component-accurate updates quickly.

What changed (high level): - Adds new required intake fields: status_component_id, impacted_region, impacted_capability - Adds opinionated defaults + "Unknown/TBD" escape hatch to avoid blocking declaration - Adds server-side validation for required fields on incident creation + updates - Writes selected values into the incident record (used later by statuspage integration + summary generator) - Adds lightweight guardrails: if severity >= Sev2 and customer-facing, require the fields; if internal-only, allow empty but warn - Normalizes naming (component_id) to align with status component registry schema being added in observability-pack

User-facing behavior (Slack modal): - New section: "Customer-facing impact" - Component: single-select list (display names) mapping to status_component_id - Region: single-select (us-east, us-west, eu-west, ap-southeast, global) - Capability: single-select (chat, streaming, embeddings, reranking, console, dedicated-control-plane) - Includes helper text + link to component taxonomy doc

Validation rules: - For Sev0/Sev1/Sev2 incidents with "Customer-facing" = true: component + region + capability must be set (can be set to "unknown" values on first pass) - For Sev3+ or "Customer-facing" = false: fields optional, but modal shows a non-blocking warning when empty - On update: disallow clearing these fields for customer-facing incidents (prevents accidental regressions during handoffs)

Rollout notes: - Landed behind feature flag incident_intake.require_status_classification (default off) - Once enabled: the workflow still allows a fast declaration via the "Unknown/TBD" options; bot posts a reminder at +15m if unknown values remain for a customer-facing incident - This PR does not publish to Statuspage; it only captures structured fields (publishing comes in PR-19344)

Testing: - Unit tests for schema validation + backward compatibility for existing incidents without these fields - Manual: ran /incident declare in #incidents-sandbox and verified modal behavior across Sev1 vs Sev3 and customer-facing toggle

Screenshots: (internal) Slack modal before/after in the PR attachments

source.title: Add Comms Lead role assignment + TTFU reminders; log status updates to #incidents
source.author: Devon Price
source.description: Context: We’ve had repeated incidents where we miss the first customer-facing update window (TTFU), and ownership of incident communications is ambiguous when the IC is deep in mitigation. This PR adds an explicit Comms Lead role to the incident-bot workflow, adds configurable reminders tied to severity (TTFU targets), and logs customer-facing update actions to #incidents for shared situational awareness and later audits. Changes: (1) Incident intake now captures/assigns a Comms Lead (default: secondary responder if present; else falls back to IC; can be reassigned). (2) Bot posts a lightweight “TTFU reminder” in the incident Slack channel if no status update has been logged within the target window (Sev0/Sev1 only by default). (3) Any status update action initiated via bot commands is echoed to #incidents with incident link + component(s) + state (Investigating/Identified/etc.) and the actor. (4) Reminders are suppressed for drills/test incidents and when the incident is marked as internal-only. Targets (configurable): Sev0=10m, Sev1=15m, Sev2=30m (Sev2 reminders disabled by default; can be enabled per team). Non-goals: This does not push to the public status page (that integration is tracked in ENG-4822); it only tracks/logs “customer-facing update” events and nudges the Comms Lead. Rollout: Behind feature flag INCIDENT_COMMS_LEAD_V1 (default off). Dogfood on SRE on-call rotation for 1 week; then expand to Infra on-call. Notes: Reminder messages are intentionally non-judgmental and include quick links to templates/runbook. Logging to #incidents avoids posting customer-specific details; the log line includes only incident ID, severity, impacted components (from intake), and phase.
commits: feat(workflow): add Comms Lead role to incident model and intake flow
feat(reminders): add severity-based TTFU reminder scheduler with drill suppression
feat(logging): echo status update events to #incidents with actor + incident link
chore(config): add comms targets + feature flag wiring
test: add unit tests for reminder timing + reassignment edge cases
docs: update /help and role definitions for Comms Lead
review_conversation: Rafael Mendes: Love the explicit Comms Lead. Two asks: (1) please ensure we don’t nudge for Sev2 by default; Sev2 noise will annoy on-call. (2) The reminder should be keyed off a bot-tracked ‘customer-facing update’ event, not just incident state transitions.

Devon Price (author): Done. Sev2 reminders are disabled by default in config; we can enable per team. Reminders now trigger only when no `customer_update_logged_at` is present (set by `/incident status-update logged` command). State transitions no longer count.

Allison Grant: For the #incidents echo, can we include a consistent prefix + fields so we can parse later? e.g. `INCIDENT_UPDATE` + incident_id + severity + phase + components. Also please avoid including raw metric numbers in the echo to keep it low-risk for public transparency work.

Devon Price (author): Added prefix `INCIDENT_UPDATE` and a stable key=value format. Echo includes incident_id, sev, phase, components, region (if set), and actor. No metrics in the #incidents message; metrics stay in the incident channel only.
Dev Patel: Please confirm this doesn’t ping #support automatically. We’ve had bot spam in the past. Also wording should be calm—no ‘SLA breach’ language.

Devon Price (author): Confirmed: only posts to the incident channel + #incidents (internal). No #support messages. Reminder copy updated to: ‘Reminder: we aim to post an initial customer-facing update within X minutes for SevY. If you have context, please log an update or note that we’re investigating.’
Rafael Mendes: One more edge case: reassigning Comms Lead mid-incident should cancel outstanding reminders for the previous assignee. Also suppress reminders when incident is resolved.
Devon Price (author): Implemented: reminder job checks latest incident record; if resolved or Comms Lead changed, it no-ops. Added tests for reassignment + resolved suppression.
Allison Grant: Approved after the key=value format change. Looks good.
Rafael Mendes: Approved. Please add a short rollout note to the PR so on-call knows how to enable the flag.
Devon Price (author): Added rollout section + flag name to description and docs.
source.release_notes: incident-bot: Added Comms Lead role assignment, severity-based TTFU reminders (flagged), and internal logging of customer-facing update events to #incidents.

source.title: Ephemeral runbook cache and idempotent rollforward handler for safe post-incident triage
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Adds ephemeral runbook caching to speed on-call lookups and an idempotent rollforward handler to safely apply automated remediations during triage. Backwards-compatible; no config changes required for existing playbooks.
source.description: Context and motivation: On high-noise incidents the incident-bot runbook lookups and remediation orchestration were adding 150-300ms per request and occasionally executing non-idempotent handlers twice when a race in the triage state machine retried a step. This PR introduces a bounded ephemeral runbook cache (in-memory with TTL and stale-while-revalidate) to reduce lookup latency, and a 'rollforward' handler wrapper that enforces idempotency and safe retries for automated remediation actions. Goals: 1) reduce median oncall lookup latency under load, 2) prevent double-applies of non-idempotent remediation steps, 3) preserve existing runbook semantics and allow opt-out via annotation. Changes summary: - runbook cache: lightweight LRU with 1s default TTL, 5s stale-while-revalidate, metrics and per-team tunables - rollforward handler: decorator that records ephemeral execution tokens in the triage transaction store and rejects duplicates or replays; supports compensating actions and safe-noop return codes - migrations: small schema bump to triage transactions to add an executions map (backfilled lazily) - safety: feature flag off by default for first week, auto-fallback to existing behavior on store errors - telemetry: tracing spans, histogram of cache hit/miss, counter for suppressed duplicate executions Why this approach: persistent caching at the platform level would add operational complexity and cross-tenant leak risk. An ephemeral per-instance cache with clear TTLs gives the latency wins while remaining safe for multi-tenant deployments. The rollforward decorator centralizes idempotency logic so individual runbook steps do not need bespoke guards. Backwards compatibility: runbooks without the new annotation keep current semantics. Opt-in: annotate runbook step with "idempotent: true" to enable the stronger protections. Rollout plan: feature-flag gated, dogfood enabled for SRE, staged rollout to 20% of instances on 2025-11-07, monitor cache hit ratio and suppressed-exec counts, ramp to 100% after 72h if no anomalies. Related work: follow-up will add persistent per-team cache and richer compensator registry (ENG-4899). || Commits summary: - 64a2b3d: initial runbook cache implementation and unit tests - 7f1c8b2: add stale-while-revalidate + metrics and config knobs - b2d98f1: rollforward handler, decorator API, and integration tests - c3e9f34: triage transaction schema bump and migration shim - d11a7e0: safety feature flag + toggles in ops console - e4f5a90: docs and runbook examples; update changelog || Files changed (high level): ["pkg/runbook/cache.go", "pkg/runbook/handler_rollforward.go", "pkg/triage/transaction_store.go", "migrations/20251103_add_exec_map.sql", "internal/flags/feature_flags.go", "docs/runbooks/idempotency.md", "pkg/runbook/cache_test.go"] || Small illustrative snippet (cache constructor): 'func NewEphemeralCache(maxEntries int, ttl time.Duration, stale time.Duration) *Cache { // LRU + TTL + stale-while-revalidate }' || Tests and CI: unit tests added for cache eviction, stale revalidate, rollforward duplicate suppression, and basic integration between triage store and handler. Local benchmarks show median lookup latency drop from 260ms to 60ms for repeated lookups under synthetic load. CI history: initial run failed 'integration-test:triage' due to a flaky test around transaction ordering; test was hardened and rerun, full CI passed on second run. || Review conversation (selected highlights): "Arjun Patel: Overall looks good, I like the simplicity of the cache. Please add a metric for suppressed-exec reasons (duplicate|stale|store-error) and ensure we log suppressed events at debug level only." "Maya Chen (author): Added suppressed_exec_reason counter and switched logs to debug. Also added comment on expected store errors path." "Lucia Gomez: Concerned about schema migration during high-traffic incidents. Can we make the migration non-blocking and lazy?" "Maya Chen: Migration is a no-op if exec_map is present; we use a lazy backfill and fall back to ephemeral token matching for old records. Added tests and docs describing migration safety." "Ethan Park: Can we prevent cache from holding sensitive runbook fragments?" "Maya Chen: Cache only stores resolved runbook metadata (IDs, step signatures, handler refs) not secrets or full payloads. Added explicit filter and unit test to validate no secret fields are cached." "Arjun Patel: Approve after CI green." "Lucia Gomez: Approve, left small nit on doc wording." || Requested changes and responses: "Request: add e2e scenario that simulates duplicated execution due to a retried triage call." "Response: added e2e in pkg/runbook/rollforward_e2e_test.go that runs triage with simulated network retry and asserts a single effective side-effect (mock compensator verifies)." "Request: make default TTL configurable via env and add a sentinel to disable cache per-team." "Response: added env var REDWOOD_RUNBOOK_CACHE_TTL and per-team annotation 'cache: disabled' respected by constructor." || Observability and telemetry added: ["runbook.cache.hits_total", "runbook.cache.misses_total", "runbook.cache.stale_revalidate_total", "runbook.rollforward.suppressed_total", "runbook.rollforward.suppressed_reason"] || Rollout/operational notes: Feature flag 'runbook.ephemeral_cache_rollforward' is off by default. Ops checklist added to docs: how to toggle, how to read metrics, how to emergency-toggle to off in case of regression. Emergency rollback is to disable feature flag which restores previous behavior instantly. || Merge outcome: Squash-merged into main after approvals and green CI. Merged_at: 2025-11-10 18:34:12 UTC.

Related work: follow-up will add persistent per-team cache and richer compensator registry (ENG-4899). || Commits summary: - 64a2b3d: initial runbook cache implementation and unit tests - 7f1c8b2: add stale-while-revalidate + metrics and config knobs - b2d98f1: rollforward handler, decorator API, and integration tests - c3e9f34: triage transaction schema bump and migration shim - d11a7e0: safety feature flag + toggles in ops console - e4f5a90: docs and runbook examples; update changelog || Files changed (high level): ["pkg/runbook/cache.go", "pkg/runbook/handler_rollforward.go", "pkg/triage/transaction_store.go", "migrations/20251103_add_exec_map.sql", "internal/flags/feature_flags.go", "docs/runbooks/idempotency.md", "pkg/runbook/cache_test.go"] || Small illustrative snippet (cache constructor): 'func NewEphemeralCache(maxEntries int, ttl time.Duration, stale time.Duration) *Cache { // LRU + TTL + stale-while-revalidate }' || Tests and CI: unit tests added for cache eviction, stale revalidate, rollforward duplicate suppression, and basic integration between triage store and handler. Local benchmarks show median lookup latency drop from 260ms to 60ms for repeated lookups under synthetic load. CI history: initial run failed 'integration-test:triage' due to a flaky test around transaction ordering; test was hardened and rerun, full CI passed on second run. || Review conversation (selected highlights): "Arjun Patel: Overall looks good, I like the simplicity of the cache. Please add a metric for suppressed-exec reasons (duplicate|stale|store-error) and ensure we log suppressed events at debug level only." "Maya Chen (author): Added suppressed_exec_reason counter and switched logs to debug. Also added comment on expected store errors path." "Lucia Gomez: Concerned about schema migration during high-traffic incidents. Can we make the migration non-blocking and lazy?" "Maya Chen: Migration is a no-op if exec_map is present; we use a lazy backfill and fall back to ephemeral token matching for old records. Added tests and docs describing migration safety." "Ethan Park: Can we prevent cache from holding sensitive runbook fragments?" "Maya Chen: Cache only stores resolved runbook metadata (IDs, step signatures, handler refs) not secrets or full payloads. Added explicit filter and unit test to validate no secret fields are cached." "Arjun Patel: Approve after CI green." "Lucia Gomez: Approve, left small nit on doc wording." || Requested changes and responses: "Request: add e2e scenario that simulates duplicated execution due to a retried triage call." "Response: added e2e in pkg/runbook/rollforward_e2e_test.go that runs triage with simulated network retry and asserts a single effective side-effect (mock compensator verifies)." "Request: make default TTL configurable via env and add a sentinel to disable cache per-team." "Response: added env var REDWOOD_RUNBOOK_CACHE_TTL and per-team annotation 'cache: disabled' respected by constructor." || Observability and telemetry added: ["runbook.cache.hits_total", "runbook.cache.misses_total", "runbook.cache.stale_revalidate_total", "runbook.rollforward.suppressed_total", "runbook.rollforward.suppressed_reason"] || Rollout/operational notes: Feature flag 'runbook.ephemeral_cache_rollforward' is off by default. Ops checklist added to docs: how to toggle, how to read metrics, how to emergency-toggle to off in case of regression. Emergency rollback is to disable feature flag which restores previous behavior instantly. || Merge outcome: Squash-merged into main after approvals and green CI. Merged_at: 2025-11-10 18:34:12 UTC.

source.title: Ephemeral playbook preview + safe-autoreply gateway for low-severity alerts
source.author: Priya Raman
source.release_notes: Adds ephemeral playbook preview and a guarded automatic reply gateway for low-severity alerts. Includes CLI helpers and docs. Opt-in feature behind incident-bot config flag.
source.body:
Motivation: On-call responders spend time drafting and validating small mitigation steps for noisy, low-severity alerts (timeouts, retries, downstream 4xx spikes). This PR introduces two related features to reduce cognitive load and MTTD: (1) an ephemeral playbook preview UI/CLI that generates a short, editable sequence of suggested remediation steps from the incident context and recent telemetry; (2) a Safe Autoreply Gateway that can optionally send gated automated replies to ticketing/chat channels for low-risk alerts (acknowledgement + recommended self-serve steps), with rate-limiting, audit trails, and kill-switches.

What it does: - Ephemeral playbook preview: incidentctl preview-playbook <alert-id> produces a compact, human-readable playbook draft (3-5 steps) derived from the runbook templates, recent traces, and cached diagnostics. Previews are ephemeral (TTL 24h) and stored encrypted in the incident-bot ephemeral store. Previews can be edited in-place from the Console or approved via CLI. - Safe Autoreply Gateway: a configurable policy engine that decides whether to auto-respond to incoming alerts. Policies are evaluated against signal heuristics (severity, noisiness, alert fingerprint frequency, recent self-serve success) and a safety checklist (no creds/ops writes, no scaling actions). Autoreplies are templated, include a short playbook excerpt and links to troubleshooting docs, and are logged with signature metadata. The gateway supports dry-run and sample modes before going live.

Design constraints & safety: - Opt-in per-service and globally disabled by default. - Rate-limits per-channel and per-alert-fingerprint. - Approval workflows: autoreplies can be auto-approved, require single reviewer sign-off, or only operate in dry-run depending on policy tier. - Revocation & audit: every autoreply has a revoke endpoint and is recorded in audit logs (KMS-backed). - Privacy: telemetry snippets included in previews are sanitized via existing redaction rules; we added an extra redaction pass for token-like strings.

Checklist: - [x] Implementation for preview generation service and CLI command - [x] Safe Autoreply policy evaluator and queue - [x] Audit/logging hooks + KMS envelope for ephemeral previews - [x] Tests: unit tests for policy rules, e2e test that runs dry-run scenario - [x] Docs: console docs + quickstart for Ops to opt in - [x] Feature flag gating and rollout plan - [x] Add metrics: autoreply.count, preview.generated, preview.accepted, preview.rejected, autoreply.revoked

Testing and rollout: We exercised the feature in a dedicated staging workspace for two weeks. Test scenarios included: noisy webhook storms, ticketing loops, and low-severity dependency failures. CI initially failed due to a flaky snapshot test (tests/test_playbook_preview_snapshot.py) which was quarantined and retried; subsequent runs passed. Rollout plan: enable in sample team via feature flag for 48h in canary; collect preview.accepted rate and autoreply.revoked as safety signals; gradual expansion to more teams if safe.

Operational notes: - CLI: incidentctl preview-playbook --alert ALRT-.... --dry-run - Console: Preview shows a diffable panel and an 'Accept & Send' button. - Safety defaults: autoreply throttled to 1 per 30m per-channel and 5 per hour per-fingerprint. - Revert: feature_flag incident.ephemeral_playbook = false disables preview generation and auto-enqueueing for autoreplies immediately.

Related work: linear tickets ENG-4231 (playbook preview generator), ENG-4302 (autoreply policy engine). This PR is intentionally focused on orchestration and safety primitives; follow-ups will add LLM-based suggestion tuning and language localization.
commits: feat(ephemeral): add preview generator service and DB model
feat(autoreply): policy evaluator and queue worker
chore(cli): incidentctl preview-playbook subcommand
test: add policy unit and e2e dry-run test
docs: add console guide and opt-in instructions
fix(test): quarantine flaky snapshot test
review_comments: Miguel Ortega: Nice separation of concerns; can we shrink the preview payload to avoid large trace embeds? Suggest we include a pointer to sampled traces instead of full snippet.
Priya Raman (author): Agreed — changed preview to include sampled_trace_url and added extra redaction pass. Payload size reduced by ~65%.
Jin Park: Safety check looks good. Please add an explicit kill-switch metric and an alert when revoke rate > 1%.
Priya Raman (author): Added autoreply.revoked metric and an alert doc in docs/ oncall/autoreply_policy.md. Also added revoke endpoint tests.
Ava Chen: CI flaky snapshot was blocking; quarantining it seems fine but add a follow-up ticket to stabilize the snapshot. APPROVED.
Merge bot: CI passing now; merging with squash.
source.notes: This PR consolidates small automation UX improvements intended to let teams safely reduce noise. Backwards compatible APIs; internal feature flag controls rollout. Follow-ups: ENG-4380 (LLM tuning), ENG-4399 (localization).

source.title: Add incident template/playbook for Provision-to-Ready (GPU provisioning) failures
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.body:
## Summary
Adds a dedicated incident template + lightweight playbook for Provision-to-Ready / Dedicated fleet bring-up failures (nodes stuck in Provisioning/Validating, mass quarantine, image/driver mismatch, join failures, NCCL smoke flakes).

This is intended to standardize the minimum info we capture in #incidents and in the incident doc so SRE/Infra/Platform can triage quickly and we can compute time-to-ready and failure-rate impacts without back-and-forth.

## Why
During the staged rollout we saw a few common patterns:
- Missing pool metadata (region/GPU type/image channel), which slowed down correlating failures to a specific rollout.
- Unclear ownership handoff (Infra vs Platform vs SRE) depending on whether the failure is “can’t join cluster” vs “validation gate failing” vs “readiness service/API regressions”.
- Links to dashboards/runbooks living in different places and being pasted inconsistently.

This PR adds:
1) A new incident template “Provision-to-Ready / Fleet Readiness failure” in incident-bot.
2) Required fields (bot enforces) for the most important scoping + debugging info.
3) One-page playbook section (first 10 minutes) with quick links and suggested owner rotations.

## Template: required fields
- Incident start time (UTC) + detection source (alert name / human report)
- Customer impact statement (Dedicated pools impacted? capacity shortfall? time-to-ready regression?)
- Affected region(s)
- Pool ID(s) / cluster(s)
- GPU type(s) (A100/H100/etc)
- Image channel + version (candidate/stable + build id)
- Current readiness states distribution (Allocated/Provisioning/Validating/Ready/Quarantined)
- Failing step(s) + top failure reason(s) (e.g., kubelet join, driver parity, NCCL smoke, perf canary)
- Rollout flags enabled? (region/pool constraints)
- Immediate mitigation chosen (pause rollout, reimage, drain/quarantine override, rollback image channel)

## Playbook: first 10 minutes
- Confirm scope using the readiness summary endpoint (counts by state + top failures)
- Check Provision-to-Ready dashboards (step latency + failure rate heatmap)
- Determine whether failure is:
  A) image bake / driver parity (Infra/Release Eng)
  B) cluster join / node config baseline (Infra)
  C) readiness service / state machine / API issues (Platform)
  D) alerting/metrics/cardinality issues (SRE/Observability)
- Apply “safe” mitigations first:
  - Pause staged rollout flag(s)
  - Stop auto-promotion (candidate->stable)
  - Quarantine only if repeatable hardware/validation failure; avoid mass quarantine until capacity impact is understood
  - Reimage a small sample and re-run validation to confirm whether image rollback is warranted

## Suggested owner rotations
- Incident Commander (IC): SRE oncall
- Primary Responder: Infra/Fleet oncall
- Secondary: Platform Control Plane oncall (readiness service)
- Consult: Release Engineering (image channel/promotion), Security (if image hardening or access control suspected)

## Notes
- Links are intentionally internal and stable (dashboards + runbook + readiness service doc). If any of these URLs change we should update the template rather than relying on manual pastes.

## Screenshots
N/A (template-only change).

## Rollout
Once merged, incident-bot will pick up the new template on next deploy (no config changes required in prod).
commits: incident-templates: add provision-to-ready failure template with required fields
incident-templates: add quick links + ownership guidance section
docs: update template index + naming consistency
chore: minor lint fixes for template YAML
source.review_conversation:
Vanessa Ortiz: Can we make sure pool identifiers are required? We lost time last week because we only had “us-east” and no pool id, and the failure mode differed between pools. Also please include “image channel + version” as required (candidate vs stable).

Aisha Bello (author): Yep — updated required fields to include pool_id(s) and image_channel + image_version/build_id. Also added a note to include rollout flags for that region/pool.

Miles Thornton: Love this. Two asks: (1) include the SLO/SLI name in required fields so we can immediately tell if this is burning error budget, and (2) add a reminder about cardinality-safe logging (don’t paste per-node IDs into alert annotations).

Aisha Bello (author): Added “Related SLO/alert name(s)” required field and a playbook bullet re: metrics/cardinality (avoid per-node ids in incident doc; use readiness summary/top failures instead).

Connor O'Brien: Can we add a checkbox for “rollout paused / kill switch enabled” and “image promotion paused” so it’s explicit in the timeline? Also, please align terminology with the staged rollout doc (candidate->stable) rather than “beta/prod”.

Aisha Bello (author): Added checkboxes for rollout pause + promotion pause. Renamed beta/prod to candidate/stable throughout.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Please add a short section on when to open a customer support escalation (if Dedicated pool is impacted) and what info Support needs.

Aisha Bello (author): Added a final section “If customer-facing” with the minimum details for Support (pool id, timestamps, impact summary, mitigation/ETA).
source.ci_checks:
Buildkite: incident-bot / unit-tests (pass)
Buildkite: incident-bot / lint-templates (pass)
Buildkite: incident-bot / validate-schema (pass)
Buildkite: incident-bot / integration-smoke (pass)
release_notes: Internal tooling: New incident-bot template for Provision-to-Ready / fleet readiness failures, including required scoping fields, quick links to dashboards/runbooks, and suggested owner rotations.

source.title: Annotate upgrade-window incidents + link to Private release artifacts
source.author: Allison Grant
source.release_notes:
- Incidents created during annotated upgrade windows now get an `upgrade_window:true` tag and (when available) `private_release_version`, `upgrade_plan_id`, and artifact links.
- Support/SRE can pivot from incident -> release artifact -> upgrade plan execution without manual correlation.
source.body:
## Summary
When a Private upgrade is running (VPC or on-prem), we want incidents that fire in that window to be clearly labeled and automatically correlated to the exact release artifacts involved.

This PR updates incident-bot’s enrichment pipeline to:
- detect upgrade windows based on a canonical source (release train + installer upgrade state signals)
- tag incidents created during those windows as upgrade-related
- attach links to relevant release artifacts (release tag, build provenance, and installer plan bundle) so on-call can quickly determine whether a spike is likely upgrade-induced vs. product regression.

This is part of the "Private upgrade + rollback mechanism" workstream focused on auditability and reduced MTTR.

## Motivation
We’ve repeatedly had to manually correlate incidents during maintenance windows:
- Was there an upgrade running?
- Which Private version / chart bundle / installer build?
- Was the incident before/after DB migrations?

This PR makes that correlation automatic and consistent, and produces a trail that Support can paste into customer comms (esp. regulated customers).

## What changed
### 1) Upgrade window detection
We enrich incidents with `upgrade_window` metadata when *either* of the following are true:
- an upgrade window annotation exists in the release calendar (time-bounded)
- an installer upgrade-state heartbeat indicates an upgrade is in progress for the environment

Precedence:
1. installer upgrade-state heartbeat (most accurate, environment-specific)
2. release calendar window (fallback; useful when signals are delayed)

### 2) Artifact linking
If we can resolve a Private release version, we attach:
- `private_release_version` (e.g. `private-2025.05.1`)
- `release_artifact_url` (release page / provenance)
- `installer_bundle_url` (where the installer fetched the upgrade plan + checksums)

If we can resolve an upgrade plan, we attach:
- `upgrade_plan_id`
- `upgrade_plan_sha256`

### 3) Tag + annotation format (stable keys)
New normalized fields on the incident annotation:
- `upgrade_window:true|false`
- `upgrade_source:installer|calendar|unknown`

title: Annotate upgrade-window incidents + link to Private release artifacts
preview:
## Summary
When a Private upgrade is running (VPC or on-prem), we want incidents that fire in that window to be clearly labeled and automatically correlated to the exact release artifacts involved.

This PR updates incident-bot’s enrichment pipeline to:
- detect upgrade windows based on a canonical source (release train + installer upgrade state signals)
- tag incidents created during those windows as upgrade-related
- attach links to relevant release artifacts (release tag, build provenance, and installer plan bundle) so on-call can quickly determine whether a spike is likely upgrade-induced vs. product regression.

This is part of the "Private upgrade + rollback mechanism" workstream focused on auditability and reduced MTTR.

## Motivation
We’ve repeatedly had to manually correlate incidents during maintenance windows:
- Was there an upgrade running?
- Which Private version / chart bundle / installer build?
- Was the incident before/after DB migrations?

This PR makes that correlation automatic and consistent, and produces a trail that Support can paste into customer comms (esp. regulated customers).

## What changed
### 1) Upgrade window detection
We enrich incidents with `upgrade_window` metadata when *either* of the following are true:
- an upgrade window annotation exists in the release calendar (time-bounded)
- an installer upgrade-state heartbeat indicates an upgrade is in progress for the environment

Precedence:
1. installer upgrade-state heartbeat (most accurate, environment-specific)
2. release calendar window (fallback; useful when signals are delayed)

### 2) Artifact linking
If we can resolve a Private release version, we attach:
- `private_release_version` (e.g. `private-2025.05.1`)
- `release_artifact_url` (release page / provenance)
- `installer_bundle_url` (where the installer fetched the upgrade plan + checksums)

If we can resolve an upgrade plan, we attach:
- `upgrade_plan_id`
- `upgrade_plan_sha256`

### 3) Tag + annotation format (stable keys)
New normalized fields on the incident annotation:
- `upgrade_window:true|false`
- `upgrade_source:installer|calendar|unknown`
- `upgrade_phase:prechecks|backup|control_plane|data_plane|migrations|rollback|unknown` (best effort)

### 4) UI-friendly output
Incident-bot posts a short, skimmable block in the incident channel:

> Upgrade context: ✅ upgrade window detected (source: installer)
> Version: private-2025.05.1
> Plan: upgplan_01J9F3… (sha256: 7b9f…)
> Artifacts: release • bundle

  - `ReleaseCalendarBackend` (reads the release train schedule exported to the annotations store)
- Adds `ArtifactLinker` to generate canonical URLs (release tag + bundle path) from version/build metadata.
- Adds a small cache (TTL 60s) to avoid hammering the annotations store during bursty incident creation.

After:

- `annotations.upgrade_plan_id = "upgplan_01J9F3K8Q..."`
