source.title: Fix timeout handling in telemetry queries (retries/backoff + better degraded-mode messaging)
source.author: Devon Price
source.description:
Context: During incidents we often burst telemetry queries (GPU util, queue depth, KV cache pressure, scheduler backpressure). When telemetry is slow or partially degraded, the bot currently either (a) times out and shows empty cards, or (b) holds the Slack interaction long enough that users think the bot is broken. This came up in INT-3341 and in recent #eng-infra / #eng-sre threads about bounding diagnostics load.

What this PR changes: 1) Adds a hard end-to-end deadline for telemetry collection per card render (default 4.5s, configurable), with per-query timeouts and cancellation propagation so we don’t keep hammering telemetry after the user path has moved on. 2) Implements bounded retries with exponential backoff + jitter for retryable classes (timeouts, 429, 502/503/504). Retries are capped (max 2) and respect the overall deadline. 3) Improves degraded-mode messaging in Slack cards when telemetry is missing/partial: instead of an empty card, we show a short reason (e.g. “Telemetry query timed out after 4.5s”) and immediately provide deep links to the relevant dashboard panels for manual drill-down. 4) Adds structured error codes in the incident context payload so downstream renderers/clients can consistently display failures (TIMEOUT, RATE_LIMITED, UPSTREAM_UNAVAILABLE, PERMISSION_DENIED, INVALID_QUERY). 5) Adds metrics and logs around retry/timeout behavior to validate we’re reducing empty-card incidence without increasing query load.

Behavior details: - Retry policy: retry only when status in {429, 502, 503, 504} or error is timeout / transient network error; do not retry on 400/401/403 or schema errors. - Backoff: base 150ms, factor 2.0, full jitter, capped at 900ms. - Deadline: total time budget includes retries; once the deadline is exceeded we return partial results + a degraded-mode banner, and emit a single structured error per missing signal group. - Slack UX: degraded-mode banner is intentionally short and does not include raw upstream error strings; we include a “View dashboards” button with pre-parameterized links (service/region/time window) and a “Copy manual query” action for on-call.

Testing: - Unit tests for retry classification and budget enforcement. - Integration test using the fake telemetry gateway to simulate a slow upstream and ensure we render partial results + banner. - Updated e2e to assert the message shown when telemetry times out.

Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Hook this into the telemetry query cache/coalescing work (ENG-5427) once that ships broadly, to avoid double-counting retries in request coalescing metrics. - Align the timeout budget with the perf canary thresholds once perf-canary/pr-221 is fully deployed.

Checklist: - [x] Added metrics: incident_bot.telemetry_query.duration_ms, incident_bot.telemetry_query.timeout, incident_bot.telemetry_query.retries, incident_bot.telemetry_query.degraded_mode - [x] Added log fields: incident_id, thread_ts, card_type, region, tenant_scope - [x] Docs: updated internal operator notes in the repo README section for diagnostics troubleshooting - [x] No customer data included in error strings; error messages are safe for #incidents / #eng-oncall
commits: telemetry: plumb cancellation + per-request timeouts through query client
telemetry: add retry policy w/ exponential backoff + jitter
bot cards: render degraded-mode banner + dashboard links when signals missing
metrics/logging: emit retry/timeout counters + structured error codes
tests: add unit + integration coverage for timeouts and retry budget
source.ci_summary: Buildkite: lint (pass), unit tests (pass), integration tests (pass), e2e (pass). One initial integration shard failure due to a flaky timer assertion; rerun passed after tightening fake clock handling in test.
review_conversation: Logan Wright: Can we ensure retries don’t blow up query volume during an active incident? Suggest hard cap + emit retry_count so we can watch it in telemetry.
Devon Price (author): Added maxRetries=2 and retry budget enforcement tied to overall deadline. Also added incident_bot.telemetry_query.retries metric (counter) and duration histogram for visibility.
Allison Grant: Slack copy should be short. Avoid leaking raw upstream errors into #incidents. Can we standardize the messages per error code?
Devon Price (author): Done. Renderer now maps TelemetryErrorCode -> fixed text. Raw error strings only go to debug logs (not Slack).
Miles Thornton: Please make sure we don’t retry on 400s (bad query) and that TIMEOUT returns partial results rather than failing the entire card.
Devon Price (author): Updated retry classifier to exclude 4xx except 429. Collector now returns partial results + banner; missing groups show as “unavailable” not blank.
Caleb Johnson: Request: add trace/log correlation ID to logs to help us join bot activity with telemetry gateway spans.
Devon Price (author): Added telemetry_request_id to log context and propagated it into the telemetry client headers.
Logan Wright: LGTM with the retry cap and structured codes.
Allison Grant: Approved. Messaging looks consistent with incident context guidelines.
Miles Thornton: Approved. This should reduce the empty-card reports we’ve been getting.
source.release_notes: Incident Bot: Telemetry-based diagnostic cards now handle slow/degraded telemetry more gracefully (bounded retries, timeouts, and clearer degraded-mode messaging with dashboard links).

source.title: Feature flag + cohort targeting for Incident Bot v2 diagnostics output
source.author: Devon Price
source.description: Summary: Introduces a gated rollout mechanism for Incident Bot v2 diagnostics (deploy context/config diffs/saturation signals) so we can run a limited beta (SRE rotation + selected regions/channels) and safely roll back to v1 output if bot latency or upstream query cost spikes during incidents. Motivation: v2 diagnostics increases upstream call volume and payload size; we need controlled exposure + a kill switch to avoid self-inflicted load during Sev events. Implementation details: Adds a new feature-flag abstraction (DiagV2Gate) with cohort targeting on Slack context (workspace, channel, user group), incident context (env/region/service), and optional allow/deny lists. Default behavior is OFF unless explicitly enabled; bot continues to render v1 cards with deep links. Adds three evaluation modes: (1) global enable/disable (INCIDENT_BOT_DIAG_V2_ENABLED), (2) cohort allowlist (INCIDENT_BOT_DIAG_V2_COHORTS), (3) per-thread override for on-call testing via a Slack interactive action "Enable v2 diagnostics in this thread" which expires automatically after a configurable TTL. Rollback/safety: Adds a hard kill switch (INCIDENT_BOT_DIAG_V2_KILL_SWITCH) that forces v1 regardless of cohort evaluation; also adds budgeted evaluation logging + metric counters to confirm flag decisions during incidents. The bot will fall back to v1 output if upstream v2 context gathering times out, but still logs the flag decision (without leaking any tenant identifiers). Telemetry: Emits metrics incident_bot.diag_v2.gate_decision{decision=enabled|disabled, reason=...} and incident_bot.diag_v2.thread_override{action=enabled|disabled}. Docs: Updates operator notes in /docs to describe how to configure cohorts and how to disable v2 during an incident. Testing: Unit tests cover cohort parsing, precedence rules (kill switch > per-thread override > denylist > allowlist > global), and TTL expiration. Includes a simple integration test that simulates message events from #incidents and validates the correct renderer path is taken. Rollout plan: Start with SRE rotation channels only (#incidents, #eng-oncall) in staging; enable prod for 10% cohort (SRE on-call user group) for one rotation; expand to platform on-call after telemetry confirms stable bot response times and no noticeable increase in diagnostics endpoint QPS. Related: ENG-5428 (rollout + feature-flagged beta), ENG-5425 (Slack interaction reliability/acks).

source.configuration_notes: New env vars: INCIDENT_BOT_DIAG_V2_ENABLED=true|false (default false); INCIDENT_BOT_DIAG_V2_COHORTS='channels=#incidents,#eng-oncall;user_groups=sre-oncall;regions=us-east-1,eu-west-1' (optional); INCIDENT_BOT_DIAG_V2_DENY='channels=#incidents-ops' (optional); INCIDENT_BOT_DIAG_V2_KILL_SWITCH=true|false (default false); INCIDENT_BOT_DIAG_V2_OVERRIDE_TTL_MIN=60 (default 60). Precedence: kill switch > per-thread override > deny > allow > global enable.
source.conversation: Miles Thornton (review): Can we guarantee v2 is fully off by default in prod even if cohorts are misconfigured? Also need a clear kill switch for on-call. Devon Price (author): Yes—global default false; cohorts are only evaluated when global is true. Added INCIDENT_BOT_DIAG_V2_KILL_SWITCH that forces v1 regardless of other config and documented it in operator notes. Rafael Mendes (review): Please add a metric for gate decisions with a reason label so we can validate cohort selection during beta. Also ensure we don't log tenant IDs in decision logs. Devon Price (author): Added incident_bot.diag_v2.gate_decision with reason={kill_switch,thread_override,denylist,allowlist,global_disabled}. Gate logs include only channel + workspace + region; no tenant identifiers. Dylan Brooks (review): Thread override action—what prevents it from persisting forever? Also make sure it doesn’t require elevated scopes beyond what we already have. Devon Price (author): Overrides stored with TTL (default 60m) and auto-expire; action uses existing Slack interactivity scope. No new OAuth scopes. Marissa Cole (review): Please confirm UX copy makes it clear this is beta and can be disabled. Devon Price (author): Updated button + confirmation text: 'Enable v2 diagnostics (beta) for this thread (expires in 60m)'. Added 'Disable v2 (beta) for this thread' action for rollback during incident. Miles Thornton (final): Looks good with precedence + kill switch. Approve after updating docs with concrete examples for cohorts string. Devon Price (author): Added examples for channels/user_groups/regions and a one-liner for emergency rollback. CI green. Merged via squash.
source.release_notes: Internal: Incident Bot v2 diagnostics can now be feature-flagged and rolled out by cohort with a kill switch for fast rollback.

title: Feature flag + cohort targeting for Incident Bot v2 diagnostics output
preview: Summary: Introduces a gated rollout mechanism for Incident Bot v2 diagnostics (deploy context/config diffs/saturation signals) so we can run a limited beta (SRE rotation + selected regions/channels) and safely roll back to v1 output if bot latency or upstream query cost spikes during incidents. Motivation: v2 diagnostics increases upstream call volume and payload size; we need controlled exposure + a kill switch to avoid self-inflicted load during Sev events. Implementation details: Adds a new feature-flag abstraction (DiagV2Gate) with cohort targeting on Slack context (workspace, channel, user group), incident context (env/region/service), and optional allow/deny lists. Default behavior is OFF unless explicitly enabled; bot continues to render v1 cards with deep links. Adds three evaluation modes: (1) global enable/disable (INCIDENT_BOT_DIAG_V2_ENABLED), (2) cohort allowlist (INCIDENT_BOT_DIAG_V2_COHORTS), (3) per-thread override for on-call testing via a Slack interactive action "Enable v2 diagnostics in this thread" which expires automatically after a configurable TTL. Rollback/safety: Adds a hard kill switch (INCIDENT_BOT_DIAG_V2_KILL_SWITCH) that forces v1 regardless of cohort evaluation; also adds budgeted evaluation logging + metric counters to confirm flag decisions during incidents. The bot will fall back to v1 output if upstream v2 context gathering times out, but still logs the flag decision (without leaking any tenant identifiers). Telemetry: Emits metrics incident_bot.diag_v2.gate_decision{decision=enabled|disabled, reason=...} and incident_bot.diag_v2.thread_override{action=enabled|disabled}. Docs: Updates operator notes in /docs to describe how to configure cohorts and how to disable v2 during an incident. Testing: Unit tests cover cohort parsing, precedence rules (kill switch > per-thread override > denylist > allowlist > global), and TTL expiration. Includes a simple integration test that simulates message events from #incidents and validates the correct renderer path is taken. Rollout plan: Start with SRE rotation channels only (#incidents, #eng-oncall) in staging; enable prod for 10% cohort (SRE on-call user group) for one rotation; expand to platform on-call after telemetry confirms stable bot response times and no noticeable increase in diagnostics endpoint QPS. Related: ENG-5428 (rollout + feature-flagged beta), ENG-5425 (Slack interaction reliability/acks).

source.title: Slack UX: modal pagination for large context + per-thread caching for repeated interactions
source.author: Devon Price
source.body:
Problem: large config diffs and saturation signal payloads frequently exceed what fits cleanly in a single Slack message block set. In practice responders were seeing (a) aggressive truncation that hid the one line they needed, and (b) repeated clicks/refreshes in a thread that re-ran expensive upstream queries, which is the opposite of what we want during an incident.

This PR introduces two changes for incident-bot v2 diagnostics rendering:

1) Modal pagination for large content (diffs + signal tables)
- Adds a "View details" button on cards that opens a Slack modal.
- Modal supports pagination (prev/next) with page size tuned for Slack's block/text limits.
- Supports both "unified" diff rendering and "key/value change list" rendering (whichever the card produced), using the same pagination component.
- Retains the existing short preview in the thread for quick scanning.

2) Per-thread caching + request coalescing for interactive actions
- Adds a lightweight cache keyed by (incident_thread_ts, card_type, query_fingerprint, view_mode, page).
- Cache TTL defaults to 120s (configurable via INC_BOT_CACHE_TTL_SECONDS); enough to cover typical repeated clicks while keeping incident context fresh.
- Coalesces concurrent identical requests (multiple responders clicking the same action) so we only hit upstream once.
- Adds "served_from_cache" + "cache_age_ms" to interaction logs to quantify impact.

Behavior / UX details:
- Thread message shows the card summary and a button row: [View details] [Refresh] (refresh bypasses cache and re-queries, still subject to existing rate limits/timeouts).
- Modal header includes service/region + time window when available.
- Pagination is deterministic: items are ordered before slicing so pages don’t reshuffle between clicks.
- If Slack modal update fails (e.g., expired trigger_id), bot replies with an ephemeral message containing a deep link to the Console / dashboard as fallback.

Performance notes:
- On repeated clicks within the TTL, we avoid rerunning telemetry queries and re-formatting large payloads.
- Worst-case (uncached): formatting + block building is now bounded per page, which reduces Slack payload size and avoids intermittent "invalid_blocks" errors.

Testing:
- Added unit tests for paginator slicing + stable ordering.
- Added integration tests around Slack interactivity payload -> modal open -> next page -> prev page.
- Added a regression test ensuring config diff previews remain redacted and pagination does not bypass redaction.

Rollout:
- Feature-flagged behind incident_bot_v2_modal_pagination (default off). When enabled, the card will include the View details button; otherwise behavior is unchanged.

Screenshots:
- (Text-only) Before: config diff card truncated after ~15 lines with "…".
- After: card shows first ~10 lines + “View details”; modal shows page 1/6 with next/prev.

Notes:
- This PR does not change upstream query budgets; it focuses on Slack rendering and interaction-level caching. Query caching at the telemetry-query-service layer is tracked separately (ENG-5427) but this adds immediate protection from repeat clicks.

Checklist:
- [x] Unit tests added/updated
- [x] Local Slack dev workspace tested (modals + pagination)
- [x] Added structured logs for cache effectiveness
- [x] Docs/comments for cache keys + TTL config
commits: slack: introduce ModalPaginator component + block builder helpers
interactions: add per-thread cache + in-flight request coalescing
cards: wire config diff + saturation signal cards to View details modal
tests: paginator ordering + slack modal next/prev actions
config: add feature flag incident_bot_v2_modal_pagination + TTL env
review_timeline: 2025-03-05 Devon Price opened PR: initial implementation of modal pagination + cache (feature-flagged).
2025-03-06 Jacob Stein: requested deterministic ordering before pagination and a regression test for redaction across pages.
2025-03-06 Devon Price: updated paginator to sort items by stable key prior to slicing; added redaction pagination regression test.
2025-03-07 Logan Wright: asked to cap cached payload size and to log cache hits/misses for visibility during incidents.
2025-03-07 Devon Price: added max_cache_entry_bytes guard (defaults 256KB) and structured logs (served_from_cache, cache_age_ms).
2025-03-08 Allison Grant: requested copy tweaks in modal header (include time window) and ensure Refresh bypasses cache but still respects rate limits.
2025-03-08 Devon Price: updated modal title/header to include window; Refresh now sets cache_bypass=true and reuses existing rate limiting path.

source.title: prioritized-rca-scoring-and-playbook-recommender
source.author: Priya Nambiar
source.description:
Motivation: on-call responders are overwhelmed by alert noise and often run a suite of irrelevant playbooks. This PR implements a lightweight root-cause scoring layer + playbook prioritizer that surfaces the most likely mitigations.

What it does (single-pass summary):
- Adds a signal fusion module that scores candidate root causes from alert metadata, recent SLO burn-rate windows (via slo-toolkit hooks), and recent deploy/rollout activity.
- Introduces a playbook prioritizer that ranks runbooks by expected mitigation probability and time-to-mitigate (TTM).
- Adds a new /incident suggest-playbook command for Slack that returns 1-3 prioritized actions and confidence bands.
- Exposes an opt-in per-service configuration to tune weights and to pin preferred playbooks.
- Includes metrics and tracing for the scoring decisions to aid audits and regression debugging.

Why this is different from previous runbook helpers: the prioritizer is probabilistic and time-aware (uses historic mitigation durations) rather than a static tag-matching engine. It is intentionally lightweight (no heavy ML infra) so it can run in the incident-bot process and be used in private deployments.

Compatibility notes: integrates with existing slo-toolkit event API (v2) and incident-bot's existing alert enrichment pipeline. Backwards compatible: if slo-toolkit data is not present the scorer gracefully degrades to metadata-only heuristics.

Testing and rollout: feature guarded behind a runtime flag; telemetry opt-in enforced per-organization. Can be enabled for a single team for staged rollout.

Files changed: high level list included below.
commit_summaries: init: add rca scoring module and interfaces
feat: playbook prioritizer and Slack command handler
chore: add slo-toolkit v2 adapter and unit tests
test: add end-to-end scenario tests with fake alerts
ci: fix lint failures and formatting
docs: add operator guide and config reference
review_comments: Omar Ruiz (review): Nice direction. A few concerns: how deterministic are the scores? we need reproducibility for post-incident audit. Can you add a seed or deterministic tie-breaker?
Priya Nambiar (author reply): Good point. I added a deterministic tie-breaker based on timestamp + canonicalized alert id. Documented in the module docstring and logged as part of decision telemetry.
Hana Svensson (review): Please make sure the SLO adapter handles missing windows gracefully. Also ping security for potential leak of deployment metadata to hosted telemetry.

Priya Nambiar (author reply): slo-toolkit adapter now returns explicit 'not_available' markers and the scorer falls back to metadata-only. Reached out to security; telemetry is opt-in and scrubbed of deploy user info. See docs/playbook-recommender.md for details.
Ethan Park (review): Tests look good, but e2e flaky on CI: test_playbook_suggestion_flow failed intermittently due to timing in mocked DB. Can we make the test less timing-dependent?
Priya Nambiar (author reply): Fixed. Reworked the test to use deterministic fake clocks and replaced sleeps with awaitable triggers. CI rerun passed.
diff_snippets:
--- a/incident-bot/src/signals/rca_scoring.py
+++ b/incident-bot/src/signals/rca_scoring.py
@@
-def score_candidates(alert, candidates):
-    # naive scoring implementation
-    return sorted(candidates, key=lambda c: c.priority, reverse=True)
+def score_candidates(alert, candidates, slo_window=None):
+    \"\"\"Fuse signals: tags, slo_window burn-rate, recent-deploys.\"\"\"
+    scores = []
+    for c in candidates:
+        s = c.priority * 0.4
+        if slo_window is not None:
+            s += slo_window.burn_rate_for(c.service) * 0.5
+        scores.append((s, c))
+    # deterministic tie-breaker: alert.id hash
+    scores.sort(key=lambda x: (x[0], -hash(alert.id)), reverse=True)
+    return [c for _, c in scores]
source.release_notes: Add probabilistic root-cause scoring and playbook prioritizer. New Slack command /incident suggest-playbook. Opt-in feature for staged rollout. No breaking changes by default.
source.tests_added: unit + e2e tests for scoring and suggestion flow
source.docs_updated: operator guide and playbook configuration reference added
source.notes_for_ops: Runtime flag: FEATURES_PLAYBOOK_RECOMMENDER=true. Config: incident-bot/config/playbook_weights.yaml per-service overrides. Telemetry opt-in controlled via ORG_TELEMETRY_OPTIN flag. When enabling, monitor metric incidentbot.playbook_suggestion.confidence and incidentbot.playbook_suggestion.latency.
qa_checklist: Unit tests for scoring logic added and passing
E2E scenario tests for Slack command in CI
Manual smoke test steps added to docs/playbook-recommender.md

source.title: deferred playbook executor with scheduled dry-run and safe rollback hooks
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description: Motivation: On-call automation needs a way to schedule runbooks to execute outside peak windows, run a non-destructive dry-run before live execution, and ensure safe automatic rollback when automation makes state changes. This PR adds a Deferred Playbook Executor that accepts scheduled windows and optional dry-run payloads, a scheduled dry-run runner that simulates playbook steps without side effects, and pluggable safe-rollback hooks that capture pre-change snapshots and provide an automated revert path. Key design points: (1) All deferred executions are stored as immutable scheduling tasks with idempotency tokens, (2) dry-run mode routes actions to a simulator layer that returns predicted outcomes and a list of mutated resources, (3) rollback hooks register resource snapshotters and revert actions, and (4) RBAC/feature-flag gating prevents accidental live scheduling. This change also adds telemetry (metrics and logs) for scheduled_task.created, scheduled_task.executed, dry_run.result, and rollback.invoked, plus tracing tags for schedule_id and runbook_id. Rollout: feature-flag gated, initial rollout to staging for 2 weeks with synthetic scheduled dry-run tests, then canary to 10% of production namespaces. See linked Linear items for acceptance criteria and SLO checks.
commits: feat(executor): add deferred scheduler and task store
feat(simulator): implement dry-run simulator layer and action stubbing
feat(rollback): pluggable rollback hook interface and built-in snapshotter for k8s resources
test: add unit + integration tests for scheduling, dry-run, and rollback paths
docs: usage + migration guide and CLI examples
review_comments: Diego Morales: Nice to see idempotency tokens; request: surface the schedule failure reason in UI events so SRE can triage quickly.
Author (Ava Chen): Added schedule_failure_reason field to the task store and included events emitted with failure metadata.
Priya Kapoor: Please add an explicit max-scheduled-tasks-per-namespace limit and a metric for throttled schedules.
Author (Ava Chen): Added config and metric scheduled_tasks.throttled_count; default limit 200 per namespace and can be overridden by admin settings.
Ethan Park: Danger: rollback snapshot size could be large for some resources — add safeguards and a TTL for snapshots.
Author (Ava Chen): Implemented snapshot TTL and size cap; large snapshots are stored in object storage with a short retention window and an audit log entry.
ci_checks: security-scan: pass with one low-risk finding (temporary credentials check)
source.release_notes: Added Deferred Playbook Executor with scheduled dry-runs, safe rollback hooks, telemetry for scheduled tasks, RBAC gating, and CLI examples. Feature-flag gated rollout.
source.notes_for_ops: When enabling the feature flag in production, ensure the scheduled_task store has been migrated and object storage credentials are configured for snapshot storage. Monitor scheduled_task.* metrics and dry_run.result outcomes during canary. If rollback.invoked rises unexpectedly, roll back the feature flag and inspect snapshot retention settings.
source.manual_migration_steps: 1) Run DB migration 20250214_add_scheduled_tasks_table.sql; 2) Deploy object-storage credentials secret 'rollback-snapshots'; 3) Enable feature flag 'deferred_playbooks' in staging; 4) Run smoke scheduled dry-run jobs from CLI for two namespaces; 5) Promote flag to canary and monitor metrics for 48 hours.
source.testing_plan: Unit tests added for scheduler, simulator, and rollback interfaces. Integration test harness simulates K8s resources and verifies dry-run returns no mutations while live run mutates resources and rollback restores state. Added e2e preflight CLI script 'tools/e2e/schedule_dryrun.sh'.
source.security_considerations: Dry-run mode prevents write operations by routing through an action simulator. RBAC checks are enforced at API layer; only users with 'runbook.schedule' permission can create deferred tasks. Snapshots stored in object storage are encrypted with customer KMS when available. Audit events emitted for schedule creation, execution, dry-run, and rollback.
source.changelog_entry: deferred-playbooks: introduce deferred playbook scheduling, scheduled dry-run simulation, and safe rollback hooks (feature-flag).
source.author_acknowledgements: Thanks to Diego for the idempotency review, Priya for operational limits guidance, and the SRE on-call team for staging validation.

title: deferred playbook executor with scheduled dry-run and safe rollback hooks
preview: Motivation: On-call automation needs a way to schedule runbooks to execute outside peak windows, run a non-destructive dry-run before live execution, and ensure safe automatic rollback when automation makes state changes. This PR adds a Deferred Playbook Executor that accepts scheduled windows and optional dry-run payloads, a scheduled dry-run runner that simulates playbook steps without side effects, and pluggable safe-rollback hooks that capture pre-change snapshots and provide an automated revert path. Key design points: (1) All deferred executions are stored as immutable scheduling tasks with idempotency tokens, (2) dry-run mode routes actions to a simulator layer that returns predicted outcomes and a list of mutated resources, (3) rollback hooks register resource snapshotters and revert actions, and (4) RBAC/feature-flag gating prevents accidental live scheduling. This change also adds telemetry (metrics and logs) for scheduled_task.created, scheduled_task.executed, dry_run.result, and rollback.invoked, plus tracing tags for schedule_id and runbook_id. Rollout: feature-flag gated, initial rollout to staging for 2 weeks with synthetic scheduled dry-run tests, then canary to 10% of production namespaces. See linked Linear items for acceptance criteria and SLO checks.

source.title: Add saturation signal queries (GPU/queue/KV cache) to incident context payload
source.author: Jacob Stein
source.release_notes: Incident Bot v2: adds saturation context (GPU util, queue depth, KV cache pressure) with bounded queries, thresholds, and dashboard deep links.
source.body:
## Summary
This PR adds a new “Saturation signals” card to the incident-bot v2 context payload and Slack rendering. The card pulls a bounded set of telemetry signals for the affected service/region over a short window (default: last 15m) and summarizes them using simple thresholding so responders can quickly decide whether they’re dealing with capacity/scheduler pressure vs. an application/deploy regression.

Signals included (initial set): (1) GPU utilization (avg/p95), (2) scheduler/ingress queue depth (p95 + max), (3) KV cache pressure (p95), plus optional backpressure/admission-control indicators when present.

This is designed to be safe under incident conditions: strict timeouts, query budget enforcement, and an explicit degraded-mode response (cached snapshot + dashboard links) when live queries fail.

## Motivation
Multiple recent incidents (e.g., EU-west capacity saturation + streaming timeouts) required responders to manually piece together GPU/queue/KV cache data across dashboards. Our “first 5 minutes” goal is to provide consistent, actionable saturation context alongside deploy/config cards. This PR implements the telemetry integration portion for ENG-5423.

## What changed
- Adds a telemetry query client wrapper with:
  - hard timeout (2.0s total, 1 retry with jittered backoff)
  - bounded query time window (15m) and fixed aggregations
  - tag allowlist (service, region, pool, route where applicable) to avoid incident-bot creating high-cardinality queries
  - per-thread request coalescing hook (wired but cache layer comes in ENG-5427)
- Adds signal definitions + thresholds:
  - GPU util highlight when p95 >= 90% or avg >= 80%
  - Queue depth highlight when p95 >= 200 or max >= 500 (unit: requests waiting; see notes below)
  - KV cache pressure highlight when p95 >= 0.85 (fraction of configured KV cache capacity)
  - Each highlight includes “why it matters” copy and a suggested next step (e.g., check routing/fallback policy, batching changes, or regional capacity)
- Adds payload shape for `saturation` context and Slack rendering:
  - A compact summary line + 3–5 key metrics
  - A “View dashboard” deep link (templated) with service/region/time window
  - “Degraded mode” messaging when queries time out (no raw errors dumped into #incidents)

## Example payload (redacted)
The bot now attaches the following fields under the incident context payload (names may evolve; see inline comments for versioning):
`saturation.window=15m; saturation.gpu_util.avg=0.77; saturation.gpu_util.p95=0.91; saturation.queue_depth.p95=245; saturation.queue_depth.max=610; saturation.kv_cache_pressure.p95=0.88; saturation.highlights=[gpu_util_high, queue_depth_high, kv_pressure_high]; saturation.links.dashboard=https://console.redwood.example/obs/saturation?service=gateway&region=eu-west&from=-15m`

## Notes / guardrails
- Queue depth metric semantics differ slightly between Hosted vs Dedicated pools today; this PR uses the shared “requests waiting” gauge that telemetry team is standardizing. We only surface a single number for now and link to the dashboard for per-component breakdown.
- If region is unknown (e.g., incident declared before we have a region hint), the card falls back to “global” aggregation and labels the output accordingly.
- This PR does not introduce tenant-scoped queries; these are service/region/pool level signals only.

## Testing
- Unit tests for query builder (ensures allowlisted tags only, fixed aggregation/window)
- Snapshot tests for Slack blocks (normal + degraded mode + missing metric cases)
- Integration test against dev telemetry endpoint (skipped in CI; runnable locally)

## Rollout
- Feature-flag gated behind `incident_bot_v2_saturation_card` (enabled in staging only by default)
- Production rollout planned as part of ENG-5428 cohort beta

## Follow-ups
- ENG-5427: Add caching/request coalescing across threads and repeated interactions
- ENG-5429: Load-shedding policy for diagnostics during telemetry brownouts

Refs: ENG-5423; related: PR-1865 (timeout handling improvements) and observability-pack dashboards PR (forthcoming).
commits: telemetry: add bounded query builder and client wrapper
bot: add saturation context model + serializer
slack: render saturation card (normal/degraded) + snapshot tests
tests: add allowlist/tag-budget enforcement coverage
docs: update internal operator notes for saturation thresholds
conversation: Allison Grant (review): Can we tighten the wording around KV cache pressure? People confuse it with GPU memory usage. Also, thresholds should be documented as “initial defaults” with a note that they’ll be tuned.
Jacob Stein (author): Updated copy to explicitly say “fraction of configured KV cache capacity used by active sequences” and added “thresholds are starting points” note in the card footer + docs.

Logan Wright (review): Please make sure we’re not letting callers pass arbitrary tags through. I see `route` included—can it blow up cardinality during incidents? Also, hard timeout should include the retry budget.

Jacob Stein (author): Locked tags to an allowlist (service, region, pool) by default; `route` is only included when route is one of the incident’s known affected routes and is capped to max 3. Timeout now covers total call budget; retry uses remaining time only.
Miles Thornton (review): Slack rendering: keep the top line compact. On-call feedback was “too verbose”. Could we make the highlights short and push guidance into an “expand” interaction later?

Jacob Stein (author): Trimmed highlight text to one-liners, moved guidance into the dashboard link tooltip text, and kept only the 3 primary metrics in the card body. We can add expand/pagination in a later PR (1854 is already working on large-card UX).
Nadia Rahman (review): Can we standardize units and label them explicitly? Queue depth vs. queue latency gets mixed up a lot.
Jacob Stein (author): Added explicit “requests waiting” label next to queue depth values and clarified units in docs/operator/incident-bot-v2.md.
Logan Wright (approval): Looks good. Thanks for the guardrails + degraded mode behavior.
Allison Grant (approval): Approved after copy/threshold notes changes.
Miles Thornton (approval): Approved. Please ensure feature flag stays off in prod until cohort beta.

source.title: Redact secrets in config diffs before rendering to Slack
source.author: Devon Price
source.description: Problem: Incident-bot v2 is starting to surface tenant-safe config diffs in #incidents and #eng-oncall, but the current formatting is purely syntactic and can leak secrets if upstream config contains inline credentials/tokens (or if a value looks like a secret). This PR adds a deterministic redaction layer that runs before any diff content is rendered to Slack (cards, modals, and copy-to-clipboard). Approach: (1) Add key-based redaction (denylist of sensitive key names + path-based matches). (2) Add value-based secret detection (JWTs, API keys, common token prefixes, PEM blocks, base64-ish long strings, AWS-like access keys, etc.) with conservative thresholds. (3) Add allowlisted keys/paths that are safe to show even if they trip generic heuristics (e.g., numeric limits, routing weights, non-sensitive feature flag booleans). (4) Replace redacted values with stable placeholders ("[REDACTED:key]" / "[REDACTED:pattern]"), preserving diff structure for incident triage. (5) Add tests ensuring we never render raw secret patterns to Slack output (unit tests + golden snapshots for Slack blocks). Also adds a visible warning banner when redaction occurs and a counter metric to help tune false positives over time. Notes: This PR does not change authorization; it is a defense-in-depth layer. It intentionally over-redacts in ambiguous cases. Follow-up: align patterns with redwood/config-service redaction hooks once that endpoint ships (ENG-5422) and wire audit events for "redaction_applied" vs "no_redaction" for easier compliance review.
commits: Add redaction pipeline for config diffs (key + value heuristics)
Introduce allowlist + path matcher for known-safe fields
Add secret pattern detectors (JWT/PEM/token prefixes) + thresholds
Slack rendering: show redaction warning + preserve diff formatting
Tests: snapshot Slack blocks + fuzz-ish cases for secret patterns
Metrics: config_diff_redactions_total + redaction_reason label
source.conversation: Miles Thornton (review): Can we guarantee that *all* Slack surfaces (thread card + modal + any “expand” action) go through the same redaction path? I don’t want a path where the card is safe but the modal leaks. Devon Price (author): Yep — pushed the formatter down so both blocks + modal call formatConfigDiff(), which always redacts. Added a test that renders both block types and asserts the raw secret strings are absent. Kaitlyn Nguyen (review): The allowlist makes me nervous. Are we allowlisting by exact key only, or by path? Also please avoid allowlisting anything that could be tenant-provided strings (names, descriptions) because those can contain accidental secrets. Devon Price: It’s path-aware (JSON pointer-ish) and limited to numeric/bool fields we already rely on for incident debugging (routing weights, quota limits, feature flags booleans). I removed “description” and “notes” from the early draft allowlist. Marcus Lin (review): Pattern detector: add PEM block coverage and be careful with base64 detection — false positives are likely on hashed IDs. Suggest thresholding on length + entropy-ish heuristic. Devon Price: Added PEM begin/end matcher + updated base64 rule: requires (a) >= 32 chars, (b) high alphabet ratio, and (c) not purely hex. Also added explicit bypass for UUID-like values. Benji Okafor (review): We should preserve diff readability. Are we redacting the entire line or only the value? Devon Price: Only the value. Keys + structural diff markers remain so you can still see *what changed*. Also added a count of redacted fields and a banner in Slack: “Some values redacted (N)”. Miles Thornton: LGTM once CI is green. (later) CI passed. Approved.
source.checks: CI (incident-bot): lint ✅, typecheck ✅, unit tests ✅, Slack snapshot tests ✅; Security hooks: secret-scan ✅ (no new secrets detected); Build: docker image ✅
source.release_notes: Incident-bot: Config diff output now redacts secret-like values and sensitive keys before rendering in Slack; adds redaction warning banner and metrics for tuning false positives.

source.title: Add deploy context card (recent deploys + canary status) to incident thread diagnostics
source.author: Devon Price
source.description: Motivation: During recent incidents we repeatedly lost time answering “what changed?” in the first 5 minutes. This PR adds a new Incident Bot diagnostics card that attaches deploy context directly into the incident thread, including recent deploys for the affected service/region, rollout phase, canary health, and commit/image identifiers. It’s designed to be tenant-safe and useful even when deeper telemetry is timing out. Scope: (1) New Slack “Recent deploys” context card rendered in the thread reply alongside existing diagnostics cards. (2) Backend integration to fetch recent deploy metadata from the release registry (service -> last N deploy events) and normalize it into the bot’s context payload. (3) Links out to the deploy timeline view and the referenced commit/PR when available. UX notes: The card summarizes up to 3 most recent deploys in the last 6 hours, then provides a “View full deploy timeline” link. Canary state is highlighted (PASS/FAIL/UNKNOWN) with the relevant % traffic and rollout step when present; missing fields degrade gracefully. Implementation details: Introduces a DeployContextCollector that calls release-metadata-service with a strict timeout (800ms) and returns partial results on timeout. Slack blocks are generated via a new deploy_context.ts renderer with truncation rules to avoid overly verbose output in #incidents. Adds unit tests for the render output and integration tests that validate we never show more than the allowed number of rows and we don’t fail the entire diagnostics payload if the release service is unavailable. Follow-ups: Feature flagging/cohort rollout is tracked separately; this PR is wiring + rendering only (it can be toggled via the existing diagnostics.enable_deploy_context config knob in dev/staging).
commits: collector: add DeployContextCollector for release-metadata-service lookups
slack: render deploy context as a dedicated Block Kit section with fields + links
tests: add render snapshot tests + degraded-mode integration test
docs: update diagnostics payload schema comments + troubleshooting note
source.checks: CI: build (pass), lint (pass), unit tests (pass), integration tests (pass), Slack Block Kit validation (pass).
source.conversation: Miles Thornton (review): Can we guarantee this never blocks the rest of the diagnostics reply? We’ve been bitten by a slow upstream causing the bot to miss Slack ack deadlines. Devon Price (author): Yes — collector is isolated and wrapped in Promise.race with an 800ms timeout. On timeout we return {status: "timeout"} and render a single-line degraded message + link. Added an integration test to assert the pipeline still returns other cards when deploy context times out. Connor O'Brien (review): The rollout phase field naming here (“canary_step”) doesn’t match what release registry emits. It’s “phase” + “stepName” in the current API. Devon Price (author): Good catch. Updated mapping in release_metadata_client.ts and adjusted fixture + renderer labels. Sahana Iyer (review): Please avoid rendering raw image tags; we prefer short SHA + link. Also make sure the commit SHA is the git SHA, not the image digest. Devon Price (author): Updated: we render image_sha_short (7 chars) and commit_sha_short (7 chars) with explicit labels, and link to the deploy record which has both. Rafael Mendes (review): Truncation rules: can we cap the card to 3 deploys, and sort most-recent-first? Devon Price (author): Done. Added ordering + maxRows=3 and a “View full deploy timeline” deep link. Miles Thornton (review): One more: if service name can’t be resolved from the incident payload, what do we do? Devon Price (author): Renderer now shows “Service: unknown” and only shows the generic deploy dashboard link; collector is skipped if service is missing. Miles Thornton (final): Approved. Please keep the wording consistent with the other cards (Title Case, short labels). Connor O'Brien (final): Approved after mapping fix.
source.release_notes: Internal: Incident Bot diagnostics now include a “Recent deploys” card (service/region, rollout phase, canary health, commit/image identifiers) to speed first-5-minute triage.

UX notes: The card summarizes up to 3 most recent deploys in the last 6 hours, then provides a “View full deploy timeline” link. Implementation details: Introduces a DeployContextCollector that calls release-metadata-service with a strict timeout (800ms) and returns partial results on timeout. Slack blocks are generated via a new deploy_context.ts renderer with truncation rules to avoid overly verbose output in #incidents. Adds unit tests for the render output and integration tests that validate we never show more than the allowed number of rows and we don’t fail the entire diagnostics payload if the release service is unavailable. Follow-ups: Feature flagging/cohort rollout is tracked separately; this PR is wiring + rendering only (it can be toggled via the existing diagnostics.enable_deploy_context config knob in dev/staging).

source.title: Add config diff collector (config-service integration) + Slack formatting
source.author: Devon Price
source.body:
Summary
This PR wires Incident Bot v2 into the new config-service diff endpoint and adds a first-pass Slack-safe rendering for config diffs. The goal is to surface tenant-safe config deltas (feature flags, routing policy, quotas/rate limits, model pins) in the first 5 minutes of an incident without responders needing to jump through manual queries.

Scope
- Adds a ConfigDiffCollector that calls config-service (internal) to fetch a bounded diff for a (tenant, service, region, time window).
- Adds basic formatting for Slack: compact summary card + optional “expand” view payload (used by later Slack modal work).
- Adds guardrails: query timeouts, size limits, allowlisted keys (with explicit “redacted/omitted” markers), and stable ordering to reduce diff noise.
- Adds audit context headers (requestor + incident thread id) so config-service can emit audit events (event emission handled in redwood PRs; this bot PR only propagates IDs).

Why
We’ve had multiple incidents where 503s/latency spikes correlated with a config push (feature flag, routing policy, rate limits) but on-call had no fast way to see what changed. This PR is the plumbing so the bot can attach “what changed recently” in a customer/tenant-safe way. This directly supports ENG-5422 and the broader “Incident bot + diagnostics improvements” initiative.

Design notes
- Endpoint: config-service Diff API (tenant-aware). We send: tenant_id (or “platform” for global), service, region, env, time_range (default: last 2h), and an allowlist profile.
- Redaction strategy: this PR implements a bot-side allowlist and masking of obviously-sensitive values (tokens/keys), but the source-of-truth redaction is expected to be enforced by config-service. Bot rendering treats anything not explicitly allowlisted as omitted and does not attempt to “best effort” show unknown fields.
- Bounds:
  - Timeout: 2.5s budget (collector) with a single retry at 200ms jittered backoff when error is network/5xx.
  - Diff size: max 50 changes rendered inline; remainder summarized as “+N more changes (open Console link)”.
  - Value length: truncates at 120 chars with suffix “…” and preserves before/after hashes when truncation occurs.
- Degraded mode: on timeout or permission failure, the card renders a short error + deep link stub (Console URL placeholder; final Console routing is handled in later PRs).

User-facing behavior (Slack)
- New “Config changes” section in the incident context payload (not yet enabled by default; gated behind a feature flag checked by the bot runtime).
- Inline diff uses a consistent, minimal format:
  - Added: "+ key = value"
  - Removed: "- key"
  - Modified: "~ key: old -> new"
  - Metadata line: tenant/service/region + time window + "source: config-service"
- If the diff contains any omitted/redacted fields, we show a warning line: "Some fields were omitted for safety. Request elevated access if needed." (copy: per DES-205 guidance, but final wording can change).

Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - allowlist filtering and stable ordering
  - truncation rules
  - timeout + retry behavior (fake client)
  - Slack rendering snapshot for representative diffs
- Manual test:
  - ran bot locally against staging config-service with a seeded config change; verified no secrets rendered and ordering remains stable.

Rollout
- Feature flagged behind incident_bot_v2_config_diff (default OFF).
- Intended initial cohort: SRE rotation only in #incidents (enforced via existing cohort gate; broader rollout in ENG-5428).

Follow-ups (not in this PR)
- Stronger redaction patterns + tests (tracked in PR-1857).
- Slack modal pagination for large diffs (tracked in PR-1854).
- Console deep link finalization + correct incident time windows (Product/Console work).

Checklist
- [x] Bound query cost (timeout + size limits)
- [x] No tenant/customer sensitive fields printed outside allowlist
- [x] Added tests for formatting + bounds
- [x] Documented failure modes in code comments

Review notes / discussion (highlights)
- Kaitlyn Nguyen: “Please ensure we don’t ever show unknown keys by accident. Allowlist should be default-deny.”
  - Devon Price: “Done. Renderer only sees allowlisted keys; everything else is summarized as omitted. Added a test that unknown keys never reach Slack payload.”
- Benji Okafor: “Can we make the output stable so diffs don’t flip-flop between runs?”
  - Devon Price: “Sorted by key path + change type. Added unit test to assert ordering.”
- Marcus Lin: “Bot should not invent redaction; rely on config-service for authoritative redaction. Bot can add ‘safety belt’ masking, but don’t treat it as compliance.”
  - Devon Price: “Agreed. Updated comments and made masking minimal (only obvious secret patterns). We still default-deny on keys.”
- Jacob Stein: “2.5s seems high for incidents when Slack actions have ack deadlines.”
  - Devon Price: “Collector runs asynchronously relative to Slack ack; still, reduced from 4s to 2.5s and added a ‘fallback to link’ error message. We’ll tune with telemetry once query cache lands.”

CI
- ✅ build
- ✅ lint
- ✅ unit
- ✅ integration: incident-bot payload schema validation

Notes
This PR is intentionally conservative on formatting; it’s meant to unblock attaching config deltas quickly while we iterate on UX and redaction hardening in follow-up PRs.
commits: Add ConfigDiffCollector client + interface for config-service diff
Implement allowlist filtering + stable ordering + truncation rules
Render Slack-safe inline diff summary + omitted/redacted warnings
Add unit tests + snapshot fixtures for diff formatting
Wire collector into incident context builder behind feature flag

source.title: Introduce adaptive pager suppression using noise profiling
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description:
Motivation: paging fatigue from repetitive low-value alerts continues to erode oncall bandwidth. This change adds an adaptive pager-suppression subsystem to incident-bot that builds and uses per-service noise profiles to decide when to suppress, group, or throttle notifications. Key goals: reduce noisy low-action pages by ~40% in a test cohort, provide clear feedback to oncall (why a page was suppressed), and make suppression policies configurable and auditable.

What this PR does: 
- Adds a lightweight noise profiler that accumulates sketch summaries of alert fingerprints (hash, signature dimensions) and computes short-window noise scores (noise_rate, dedupe_entropy, throttle_suggestion).
- Implements an adaptive suppression policy engine that can: suppress (no page), group (single consolidated page for an incident burst), or debounce (delay page by Xs) based on policy rules and the noise score. Policies are evaluated per-service and fall back to org defaults.
- Adds a user feedback loop: suppressed pages include a one-click feedback link which updates the profiling signals (helpful/not-helpful) and is exposed to SRE dashboards.
- Integrates the profiler with slo-toolkit so teams can import noise budgets and weight suppression aggressiveness by SLO risk.
- Exposes runtime metrics and trace hooks to aid debugging and post-incident analysis.
- CLI flags and runtime config documented for ops teams, with safe opt-out (disable suppression per service).

Checklist: 
- [x] Design doc linked in PR (Linear ENG-2914)
- [x] Unit tests for policy engine and profiler aggregation
- [x] e2e smoke test exercising suppression path + feedback loop
- [x] Metrics (Prometheus) and tracing instrumentation added
- [x] Docs: incident-bot README + onboarding note in docs/incidents/

Notes: the algorithm deliberately uses approximate sketches to bound memory and keep profiler replica-safe. This PR is conservative about default aggressiveness and ships with a 30-day telemetry-only mode before auto-suppression is engaged (configurable).

Related links: Linear ENG-2914, RFC: /docs/rfcs/2024/noise-profiling.md, internal dashboard: /dashboards/ops/noise-profile-summary

Release notes: see release_notes field.
commits: a3b9f21: feat(profiler): add sketch-based noise aggregator
4de8b77: feat(pager): implement adaptive suppression policy engine
c12d9fa: perf: batch profiler updates to reduce write QPS
ef72a4b: tests: add unit + e2e cases for suppression and feedback loop
b594c33: docs: document runtime flags and opt-out per-service
review_comments: Priya Nair: Nice approach. Can you add a brief note on how the sketch parameters were chosen and whether they adapt to cardinality?
Ava Chen: Responded - added comments and a short helper to autoscale sketch buckets based on rolling cardinality; leaving defaults conservative.
Elliot Brooks: Please ensure the feedback link does not leak any PII; verify telemetry scrubber runs before storing signals.
Ava Chen: Confirmed - scrubber added to feedback pipeline and tests updated.
Samir Patel: CI flaked on lint on first run; rerun resolved it. Approving after e2e green.
Maintainer (Roberto Diaz): Merged via squash after approvals.
source.release_notes:
- Adds adaptive pager suppression using noise profiling.
- Integrates suppression decisions with SLO noise budgets (via slo-toolkit).
- New metrics: incidentbot_noise_rate, incidentbot_suppressed_pages_total, incidentbot_feedback_ratio.
- Safe-default: starts in telemetry-only mode for 30 days unless enabled.

Ops: Use charts/incident-bot/values.yaml to opt into suppression per-service.
